meta.title: Reference ranges — population distributions + risk-optimal anchors
meta.note: population_mean/sd are on the scale given (linear or natural-log); optimal.low/high are always in raw measurement units (matching units).

fasting_glucose.population_source: NHANES 2017-2020 fasting subsample, US adults >=20y; mean fasting plasma glucose ~103 mg/dL. CDC NHANES laboratory data; consistent with Wang et al. 2021 JAMA prevalence analyses (PMID 34962530).

optimal.rationale: Lowest all-cause and CVD mortality observed in the ~80-90 mg/dL range in large cohorts; risk rises steadily above 90-100 and there is a modest J-shaped uptick below ~70-75 mg/dL.
optimal.source: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration, Lancet 2010 (PMID 20609967); Selvin et al. 2010 NEJM (PMID 20335584)

fasting_glucose.note: Diagnostic cut-points: prediabetes 100-125, diabetes >=126 mg/dL. Population SD is whole-population including diabetics; in normoglycemic adults SD is narrower (~8-10 mg/dL).

fasting_insulin.population_source: NHANES 2007-2010 non-diabetic adults; geometric mean fasting insulin ~8-9 uIU/mL (ln ~2.2), SD of ln ~0.6. Derived from Reaven-type distributions and NHANES insulin data (Li et al. 2013, Diabetes Care, PMID 23testimate); approximate.

optimal.rationale: Linear-scale ~3-6 uIU/mL corresponds to high insulin sensitivity and lowest cardiometabolic risk; risk rises monotonically with higher insulin (per_log_sd HR for CVD).
optimal.source: Conventional insulin-sensitivity literature; HOMA-IR optimum (Gast et al. 2012 meta-analysis, PMID 23226556)

fasting_insulin.note: Assayed log-transformed (per_log_sd). Fasting insulin assays are poorly standardized across labs/RIA vs immunoassay; population_mean/SD on natural-log scale are approximate. Linear geometric mean ~9 uIU/mL.

homa_ir.population_source: NHANES 1999-2010 adults without diabetes; mean HOMA-IR ~2.2-2.5 with right-skewed distribution. Sumner & Cowie 2008 (PMID 18804279) and NHANES-derived estimates.

optimal.rationale: HOMA-IR < ~1.5-2.0 indicates preserved insulin sensitivity; cardiometabolic and frailty risk rises across tertiles/quartiles above this. No meaningful lower-bound risk.
optimal.source: Gast et al. 2012 meta-analysis of HOMA-IR and CHD (PMID 23226556)

homa_ir.note: Composite index (glucose x insulin). Heat map uses per_sd on the raw index so reported on linear scale, but distribution is strongly right-skewed; SD is approximate and many analyses log-transform HOMA-IR. Threshold for insulin resistance varies 1.9-2.9 by population.

hba1c.population_source: NHANES 2017-2020 US adults >=20y; mean HbA1c ~5.6%. CDC NHANES; consistent with Selvin et al. NHANES analyses (PMID 19299318).

optimal.rationale: Lowest all-cause/CVD mortality and dementia risk in the ~5.0-5.4% range; risk rises above 5.5-5.7 (prediabetes) and a J-shaped uptick appears below ~5.0% (partly reverse causation, anemia, frailty).
optimal.source: Selvin et al. 2010 NEJM (PMID 20335584); Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration

hba1c.note: Diagnostic: prediabetes 5.7-6.4%, diabetes >=6.5%. Confounded by hemoglobinopathies, anemia, RBC turnover. Population SD includes diabetics; normoglycemic SD ~0.3-0.4%.

ogtt_2h_glucose.population_source: DECODE study and NHANES OGTT subsamples; mean 2-h post-load glucose ~105-115 mg/dL in general adult populations. DECODE Study Group 2001 (PMID 11679443).

optimal.rationale: Normal glucose tolerance is 2-h glucose < 140 mg/dL; lowest mortality observed below ~120 mg/dL. Conventional reference interval used as optimal anchor since outcome data are categorical (NGT vs IGT vs diabetes).
optimal.source: DECODE Study Group, Lancet 1999 / Arch Intern Med 2001 (PMID 11679443); ADA diagnostic criteria

ogtt_2h_glucose.note: Heat-map HRs are categorical only (NGT/IGT/diabetes), so no per-SD outcome basis. Population SD approximate; OGTT 2-h glucose is highly variable and procedure-dependent (75 g load).

ogtt_2h_insulin.population_source: Hyperinsulinemia literature; 2-h post-load insulin in non-diabetic adults median ~40-60 uIU/mL, strongly right-skewed. No single standardized large-cohort source. Approximate from Hayashi et al. 2013 and assorted OGTT cohorts.

optimal.rationale: Lower 2-h insulin indicates better insulin sensitivity; highest mortality in top quintile of post-load insulin. Conventional 'normal' upper bound ~50-60 uIU/mL used as anchor.
optimal.source: Hayashi et al. 2013 (post-load insulin and incident diabetes); outcome data quintile-based only

ogtt_2h_insulin.note: No well-standardized large general-population mean/SD exists; assay-dependent and highly right-skewed. Mean/SD are best-estimate placeholders flagged unverified.

cgm_mean_glucose.population_source: Shah et al. 2019, JCEM, CGM in non-diabetic adults: mean glucose ~98-100 mg/dL, SD ~10-12 mg/dL (PMID 31237275).

optimal.rationale: Non-diabetic CGM mean glucose centers ~90-100 mg/dL; lower mean within the normoglycemic range associated with lower CVD risk (per_sd HR). Values 70-85 are physiologic in lean fit individuals.
optimal.source: Shah et al. 2019 JCEM (PMID 31237275); CGM consensus reference ranges

cgm_mean_glucose.note: Population reference is non-diabetic adults wearing CGM; sensor calibration differs from venous plasma. SD here is between-person SD of the mean, not within-person glycemic variability.

cgm_glucose_variability.population_source: Shah et al. 2019 JCEM CGM in non-diabetic adults: glucose CV ~15-18%, SD-of-glucose ~17-20 mg/dL (PMID 31237275).

optimal.rationale: Consensus stability target is CV < 36% for diabetes; non-diabetic adults sit at CV ~15-18%, and lower variability is associated with better outcomes. Conventional/observed normoglycemic range used.
optimal.source: Battelino et al. 2019 CGM consensus (PMID 31177185); Shah et al. 2019

cgm_glucose_variability.note: Outcome HRs are categorical only. Reported as CV%; if expressed as SD of glucose (mg/dL) the population mean is ~18, SD ~5. Confirm which metric the dashboard uses.

cgm_time_in_range.population_source: Shah et al. 2019 JCEM: non-diabetic adults spend ~94-96% of time in 70-140 mg/dL (PMID 31237275).

optimal.rationale: Higher time-in-range is protective (per_unit HR, protective direction). Healthy non-diabetic adults achieve >=95% TIR in the tight 70-140 range; >=70% in 70-180 is the diabetes consensus target.
optimal.source: Battelino et al. 2019 CGM consensus (PMID 31177185)

cgm_time_in_range.note: TIR definition depends on the chosen range (70-140 for non-diabetic vs 70-180 diabetes consensus). Mean/SD here assume the non-diabetic 70-140 mg/dL window; ceiling effect makes the distribution left-skewed.

cgm_postprandial_auc.units: mg/dL x min (incremental AUC, standardized meal)

optimal.rationale: No standardized population distribution; incremental postprandial AUC depends entirely on meal composition, size and measurement window, which are not standardized across studies.

cgm_postprandial_auc.note: Although the heat map uses per_sd, there is no single general-population mean/SD because postprandial glucose AUC is meal- and protocol-dependent. Cannot source without specifying the standardized meal challenge; mean/SD left null.

optimal.rationale: No outcome data (empty outcomes block) and no standardized general-population distribution for dawn-phenomenon magnitude.

cgm_dawn_phenomenon.note: Not applicable: derived CGM feature with no outcome hazard ratios and no published population mean/SD. Magnitude is highly dependent on definition of nadir window and waking time.

fructosamine.population_source: Selvin et al. 2014, Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol, ARIC study non-diabetic adults: fructosamine mean ~230-250 umol/L (PMID 24622671).

optimal.rationale: Conventional non-diabetic reference interval ~205-285 umol/L; lower values within range track lower glycemia. Outcome data are quartile-based, so the reference interval is used as the optimal anchor.
optimal.source: Selvin et al. 2014 (PMID 24622671); standard laboratory reference intervals

fructosamine.note: Reflects ~2-3 week glycemia. Confounded by serum albumin and protein turnover; assay reference ranges vary by lab. Population SD approximate.

c_peptide.population_source: NHANES 1999-2004 fasting C-peptide, non-diabetic US adults: mean ~2.0-2.5 ng/mL, right-skewed. Derived from NHANES; consistent with Pikkemaat et al. and Patel et al. analyses.

optimal.rationale: Lower fasting C-peptide within the normal range reflects lower endogenous insulin secretion and better insulin sensitivity; mortality/CVD risk rises across upper quartiles. Standard fasting reference ~0.8-3.1 ng/mL.
optimal.source: Patel et al. 2012 (fasting C-peptide and mortality, PMID 22210570); outcome HRs quartile-based

c_peptide.note: Right-skewed; assay-dependent. Population mean/SD approximate. Interpretation differs in renal impairment (reduced clearance raises C-peptide).

adiponectin.population_source: General-population adiponectin distributions (e.g., MESA, Framingham): geometric mean ~8-9 ug/mL (ln ~2.1), SD of ln ~0.5-0.6. Approximate from pooled cohort data (Kizer et al. 2013, PMID 23446174).

optimal.rationale: Higher adiponectin is generally cardioprotective and associated with insulin sensitivity (linear ~10-20 ug/mL); however very high adiponectin shows a paradoxical positive association with all-cause mortality and frailty in the elderly, so an upper bound is given.
optimal.source: Kizer et al. 2013 (adiponectin paradox, PMID 23446174); Wannamethee et al. 2013

adiponectin.note: Assayed log-transformed (per_log_sd). Population_mean/SD on natural-log scale; linear geometric mean ~8-9 ug/mL. The 'adiponectin paradox' (high levels predict mortality/frailty in elderly) means direction is u_shaped/context-dependent rather than simply higher-better.

leptin.population_source: NHANES and general-population leptin data: strongly sex-dependent, geometric mean ~7 ng/mL men, ~20 ng/mL women, pooled ln ~2.3-2.5 with SD of ln ~0.8-1.0. Approximate (Ruhl & Everhart NHANES analyses).

optimal.rationale: Lower leptin (linear ~3-10 ng/mL) reflects lower adiposity and lower CVD/cancer risk; however very low leptin in elderly relates to frailty/wasting and a protective association exists for dementia, so the trough is intermediate.
optimal.source: Outcome HRs categorical/tertile (CVD/cancer risk) and per_log_sd (dementia, protective); leptin tracks adiposity

leptin.note: Assayed log-transformed (per_log_sd for dementia). Strongly sex- and adiposity-dependent; a single pooled mean/SD is a coarse approximation - ideally stratify by sex. Population_mean/SD on natural-log scale.

leptin_adiponectin_ratio.population_source: Derived composite; general-population leptin:adiponectin ratio median ~1-3, strongly right-skewed and sex-dependent. No single standardized large-cohort mean/SD; approximate from cohort studies (Fruhbeck et al. 2018 review).

optimal.rationale: Lower ratio indicates favorable adipose function and insulin sensitivity; CVD risk rises with higher ratio (per_sd HR). Ratio < ~1 commonly cited as insulin-sensitive.
optimal.source: Fruhbeck et al. 2018 (leptin:adiponectin ratio as insulin-resistance marker, PMID 30441846)

leptin_adiponectin_ratio.note: Composite ratio of two markers; no standardized general-population SD exists and the distribution is heavily right-skewed and sex-dependent. Heat map uses per_sd, but mean/SD here are best-estimate and flagged unverified - many analyses log-transform this ratio.

apob.population_source: NHANES 2005-2016 / Framingham ApoB distributions, US adults: mean ~90-100 mg/dL, SD ~25 mg/dL. Consistent with Wilkins et al. and NHANES apolipoprotein data.

optimal.rationale: ApoB is causal for ASCVD with monotonic risk; no lower-bound harm. Optimal/low-risk ApoB is < ~65 mg/dL, with primary-prevention targets ~60-80 and very-high-risk targets < 65; population trough risk is at the lowest attainable levels.
optimal.source: Sniderman et al. 2019 (ApoB and ASCVD, PMID 30694319); Mendelian randomization (Ference et al. 2019, PMID 31504418)

apob.note: Population SD includes treated/untreated adults. ApoB counts atherogenic particle number; preferred over LDL-C when discordant.

ldl_c.population_source: NHANES 2017-2020 US adults >=20y: mean LDL-C ~110-113 mg/dL, SD ~33-35 mg/dL. CDC NHANES lipid data.

optimal.rationale: LDL-C is causally atherogenic with log-linear risk and no demonstrated lower threshold of harm; lowest CVD risk at the lowest attainable LDL-C. Outcome/MR data support optimal < ~70 mg/dL, with physiologic 'native' LDL-C ~20-40 mg/dL.
optimal.source: Ference et al. 2017 (LDL-C causal MR, PMID 28444290); CTT Collaboration 2010 (PMID 21067804)

ldl_c.note: Friedewald-estimated LDL-C is inaccurate at high TG / low LDL; direct or Martin-Hopkins estimation preferred. Population SD includes statin-treated adults.

ldl_p.population_source: MESA cohort NMR LDL particle number, general adults: mean ~1300-1400 nmol/L, SD ~400 nmol/L (Otvos et al. / MESA, PMID 21392724).

optimal.rationale: LDL-P reflects atherogenic particle number; lower is better with monotonic CVD risk (per_sd HR). Low-risk/optimal LDL-P is < ~1000 nmol/L (roughly 20th percentile), analogous to optimal ApoB.
optimal.source: Cromwell et al. 2007 Framingham Offspring (LDL-P and CVD, PMID 17719681); MESA

ldl_p.note: NMR LipoProfile assay-specific; values not interchangeable with ion-mobility particle counts. Population mean/SD approximate, MESA-derived.

ldl_size.population_source: Framingham Offspring and MESA NMR LDL size: mean ~20.8-21.2 nm, SD ~0.6-0.8 nm (Kuller et al. / MESA NMR data).

optimal.rationale: Larger LDL particle size (pattern A) is associated with lower CVD risk than small dense LDL (pattern B); higher quartiles of size are lower-risk. Larger size is favorable but largely a marker of low TG / good metabolic health.
optimal.source: Outcome HRs quartile-based; small dense LDL phenotype literature (Austin et al. 1988; St-Pierre et al. 2005, PMID 15528474)

ldl_size.note: LDL size is largely a correlate of triglycerides/insulin resistance and adds little beyond LDL-P/ApoB once particle number is known. Assay-dependent (NMR vs gradient gel). Population mean/SD approximate.

hdl_c.population_source: NHANES 2017-2020 US adults >=20y: mean HDL-C ~53 mg/dL (men ~48, women ~58), SD ~15-16 mg/dL. CDC NHANES lipid data.

optimal.rationale: U-shaped: lowest mortality at HDL-C ~55-80 mg/dL; both low HDL-C and very high HDL-C (>~90-100 mg/dL) are associated with increased all-cause and CVD mortality. HDL-C is not causally protective per Mendelian randomization.
optimal.source: Madsen et al. 2017 Eur Heart J (extreme HDL and mortality, PMID 28637263); Voight et al. 2012 MR (PMID 22607825)

hdl_c.note: Strongly sex-dependent (women run ~10 mg/dL higher). Heat map lists CVD direction as protective (per_sd) but very high HDL-C is harmful, hence u_shaped overall.

hdl_efflux.population_source: Rohatgi et al. 2014 NEJM (Dallas Heart Study) and Saleheen et al. 2015: cholesterol efflux capacity normalized to a pooled reference, mean ~1.0, SD ~0.2 (PMID 25404125).

optimal.rationale: Higher cholesterol efflux capacity is inversely associated with incident CVD independent of HDL-C (per_sd, protective). Higher quartiles are lower-risk; optimal is the upper part of the distribution.
optimal.source: Rohatgi et al. 2014 NEJM (PMID 25404125); Saleheen et al. 2015 (PMID 26025389)

hdl_efflux.note: Functional assay with no standardized absolute units; values are normalized to a lab-specific pooled serum reference, so the mean is ~1.0 by construction and SD is assay-dependent. Not interchangeable across laboratories.

triglycerides.population_source: NHANES 2017-2020 fasting US adults: geometric mean triglycerides ~105-115 mg/dL (ln ~4.65-4.75), SD of ln ~0.5-0.6. CDC NHANES lipid data; TG distribution is strongly right-skewed and routinely log-analyzed.

optimal.rationale: Linear ~30-75 mg/dL. Lower fasting TG associated with lower remnant cholesterol and CVD risk (per_log_sd HR); optimal is well below the conventional <150 mg/dL cut. Causality via remnant particles supported by MR.
optimal.source: Nordestgaard & Varbo 2014 Lancet (triglycerides and CVD, PMID 24993914); ADA/AHA TG guidance

triglycerides.note: Assayed log-transformed (per_log_sd for CVD). Population_mean/SD on natural-log scale; linear geometric mean ~110 mg/dL. Highly sensitive to recent food intake and alcohol; requires 8-12 h fast.

tg_hdl_ratio.units: ratio, mg/dL TG per mg/dL HDL-C
tg_hdl_ratio.population_source: Derived from NHANES fasting TG and HDL-C distributions: linear TG/HDL ratio geometric mean ~2.0-2.2 (ln ~0.7-0.8), SD of ln ~0.6-0.7. Approximate; ratio is right-skewed and routinely log-analyzed.

optimal.rationale: Linear ratio ~0.7-1.5. Low TG/HDL ratio is a strong surrogate for insulin sensitivity and low small-dense-LDL; ratio < ~2 (mg/dL units) indicates low cardiometabolic risk. Risk rises monotonically with higher ratio.
optimal.source: McLaughlin et al. 2005 (TG/HDL and insulin resistance, PMID 15738451)

tg_hdl_ratio.note: Composite ratio assayed log-transformed (per_log_sd for CVD). UNITS MATTER: in mg/dL the optimal cut is ~2; in mmol/L it is ~0.9. Population_mean/SD on natural-log scale of the mg/dL ratio and are approximate (derived, not a single published cohort statistic).

lp_a.population_source: UK Biobank and general-population Lp(a): highly right-skewed, median ~20-30 nmol/L, with broad spread; ln-scale mean ~3.0, SD ~1.3-1.6. Patel et al. 2021 UK Biobank (PMID 33591808).

optimal.rationale: Linear < ~30 nmol/L (~<14 mg/dL). Lp(a) is causally atherogenic with risk rising above ~75 nmol/L; lower is better with no lower-bound harm. Optimal is the lowest decile.
optimal.source: Patel et al. 2021 UK Biobank (PMID 33591808); Burgess et al. 2018 MR (PMID 30002386)

lp_a.note: Assayed log-transformed (per_log_sd). Extremely right-skewed and largely genetically determined; population_mean/SD on natural-log scale and approximate. nmol/L (molar) and mg/dL (mass) units are NOT interconvertible by a fixed factor - confirm assay units.

non_hdl_c.population_source: NHANES 2017-2020 US adults >=20y: non-HDL-C (total cholesterol minus HDL-C) mean ~140-145 mg/dL, SD ~37-40 mg/dL. CDC NHANES lipid data.

optimal.rationale: Non-HDL-C captures all atherogenic (apoB-containing) lipoproteins; monotonic CVD risk with no lower-bound harm. Optimal/low-risk is < ~100 mg/dL (very-high-risk guideline target), with population trough at the lowest attainable.
optimal.source: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration 2009 JAMA (PMID 19903920); ESC/EAS 2019 dyslipidaemia guidelines

non_hdl_c.note: Non-HDL-C ~= LDL-C + ~30 mg/dL when TG normal; tracks ApoB closely. Population SD includes treated adults.

remnant_cholesterol.population_source: Copenhagen General Population Study and NHANES-derived remnant cholesterol (total - LDL-C - HDL-C): mean ~20-25 mg/dL, right-skewed. Varbo et al. 2013 (PMID 23083789).

optimal.rationale: Remnant cholesterol is causally atherogenic (MR-supported) with monotonic risk; lower is better. Optimal/low-risk < ~15-20 mg/dL; population trough at the lowest attainable.
optimal.source: Varbo et al. 2013 JACC (remnant cholesterol causal in IHD, PMID 23083789)

remnant_cholesterol.note: Calculated (total cholesterol - LDL-C - HDL-C); accuracy depends on the LDL-C estimation method and is poor at high TG. Right-skewed; population mean/SD approximate.

oxidized_ldl.population_source: Holvoet et al. and MESA using the Mercodia 4E6 oxidized-LDL ELISA: general-population mean ~45-55 U/L, SD ~15-18 U/L (PMID 18757281).

optimal.rationale: Higher oxidized LDL is associated with incident CVD (median-split HR); lower is better. Optimal is the lower part of the distribution. No outcome-defined lower threshold of harm.
optimal.source: Holvoet et al. 2008 (oxidized LDL and metabolic syndrome/CVD, PMID 18757281)

oxidized_ldl.note: Heat-map HR is median-split only (no per-SD outcome basis). Strongly assay-dependent (4E6 monoclonal antibody ELISA); absolute values not comparable across kits. Population mean/SD approximate.

optimal.rationale: Optimal/lowest-risk CAC is 0 (no detectable coronary calcium). Risk rises stepwise across categories (1-99, 100-399, >=400 Agatston).
optimal.source: MESA CAC reference (Budoff et al. 2007; McClelland et al. 2006, PMID 16401776)

cac_score.note: Not a normally distributed quantitative measure: CAC has a large zero-inflated spike (most adults <55y score 0) and an extreme right tail, so a population mean/SD is not meaningful and cannot be placed on a per-SD scale. Interpreted by categories or age/sex percentiles (MESA calculator). Heat-map HRs are categorical.

cimt.population_source: MESA and Carotid Atherosclerosis Progression Study reference data: mean common-carotid IMT in middle-aged adults ~0.65-0.75 mm, SD ~0.15-0.17 mm; strongly age-dependent (PMID 17242284).

optimal.rationale: Lower cIMT indicates less subclinical atherosclerosis; CVD/dementia risk rises with higher cIMT (per_sd HR). Optimal is the lower part of the age-adjusted distribution (roughly < 25th percentile).
optimal.source: Lorenz et al. 2007 meta-analysis (cIMT and CVD, PMID 17242284)

cimt.note: Strongly age-dependent - a single all-age mean/SD is coarse; ideally use age/sex-specific percentiles. Highly protocol- and sonographer-dependent (which carotid segment, near vs far wall). Mean/SD approximate, middle-aged-adult anchored.

optimal.rationale: Categorical genotype, not a measurable continuous quantity. Lowest-risk genotype is e3/e3 (or e2 carriage for CVD/dementia, though e2 raises type III hyperlipoproteinemia risk); e4/e4 is highest risk.
optimal.source: Lambert et al. / IGAP and Bennet et al. 2007 JAMA (ApoE and CHD, PMID 17848651)

apoe_genotype.note: Not a quantitative biomarker: ApoE is a discrete genotype with no population mean/SD and cannot be placed on a per-SD scale. Risk is conferred categorically (e4 dose for dementia/CVD risk; e2 protective for dementia but raises some dyslipidemia risk).

vo2max.population_source: Kaminsky LA et al. 2022, FRIEND registry treadmill normative data, ~13,000 US adults; representative value for ages 40-49 (men ~37, women ~30, midlife midpoint ~33). PMID: 34543650 (Mayo Clin Proc 2022;97:285-293)

optimal.rationale: Mortality declines monotonically with fitness; risk plateaus around the top tertile. ~42 mL/kg/min approximates >=1 SD above the midlife mean and the high-fit category beyond which incremental mortality benefit flattens.
optimal.source: Mandsager K et al. 2018, JAMA Netw Open, 122,007 patients, treadmill CRF and mortality. PMID: 30646252; Kaminsky 2022 percentiles

vo2max.note: Strongly age- and sex-dependent: men run ~7 mL/kg/min higher than women; mean falls ~8-10% per decade. Mean/SD shown is a midlife (40-49) both-sex midpoint; use age/sex-specific FRIEND percentiles for individual placement.

ventilatory_thresholds.population_source: Approximate: VT1 typically occurs at ~50-60% of VO2max in untrained adults; no single large general-population normative registry equivalent to FRIEND exists for ventilatory thresholds. Wasserman/CPET reference literature.

optimal.rationale: Higher VT1/VT2 as a fraction of VO2max indicates greater sustainable aerobic capacity; trained individuals reach VT1 at 65-85% VO2max. Conventional performance reference, not outcome-derived.
optimal.source: ATS/ACCP Statement on Cardiopulmonary Exercise Testing 2003, Am J Respir Crit Care Med. PMID: 12524257

ventilatory_thresholds.note: Reported as VT1/VT2 (absolute mL/kg/min or %VO2max or workload). No large general-population mean/SD registry; values are approximate from CPET reference texts. Outcome data are categorical only (low vs high anaerobic threshold in HF/preoperative populations).

resting_metabolic_rate.population_source: Representative midlife adult value derived from indirect-calorimetry reference data; both-sex midpoint (men ~1650-1700, women ~1300-1400 kcal/day). Johnstone AM et al. 2005, Am J Clin Nutr 82:941-948 (factors affecting RMR). PMID: 16280423

optimal.rationale: U-shaped/risk direction: higher mass-independent RMR predicts increased mortality (accelerated metabolic aging), but very low RMR reflects low lean mass/frailty. Conventional mid-range; not a clean trough.
optimal.source: Ruggiero C et al. 2008, J Clin Endocrinol Metab, BLSA cohort, higher RMR predicts mortality. PMID: 18029471

resting_metabolic_rate.note: RMR is strongly driven by lean mass, body size, age and sex; absolute kcal/day is not meaningfully interpretable without adjusting for fat-free mass. The mortality signal is for mass-adjusted RMR. Treat as approximate.

substrate_utilization.population_source: Maximal fat oxidation (MFO) ~0.4-0.6 g/min in general adults. Maunder E et al. 2018, Sports Med review of MFO determinants; Randell RK et al. 2017, Med Sci Sports Exerc, MFO in 1121 adults. PMID: 27580152

optimal.rationale: Higher maximal fat oxidation reflects greater metabolic flexibility and mitochondrial capacity; trained/insulin-sensitive individuals reach 0.6-1.0 g/min. Conventional metabolic-flexibility reference, not outcome-derived.
optimal.source: Maunder E et al. 2018, Sports Med 48:2003-2014. PMID: 29873044

substrate_utilization.note: No outcome HRs in the heat map for this marker. RER and MFO are distinct measures; MFO mean/SD shown. Highly dependent on exercise intensity, diet and training status. Approximate.

exercise_economy.population_source: Energy cost of usual-pace walking ~0.20-0.26 mL O2/kg/m in healthy older adults. Schrack JA et al. 2012, J Am Geriatr Soc, Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging, energy cost of walking. PMID: 23078218

optimal.rationale: Lower oxygen cost of walking indicates better movement economy and lower mobility-decline risk; trained/efficient walkers fall well below 230. Risk direction: higher cost predicts mobility decline.
optimal.source: Schrack JA et al. 2012, J Am Geriatr Soc 60:1811-1816. PMID: 23078218

exercise_economy.note: Walking economy rises (worsens) with age. Mean/SD is a representative older-adult value; running economy uses different units. Treat as approximate; methods and standardized speeds vary across studies.

lactate_threshold.population_source: Lactate threshold occurs at ~50-60% VO2max in untrained adults. Faude O et al. 2009, Sports Med, review of lactate threshold concepts. PMID: 19453205

optimal.rationale: Higher lactate threshold as a fraction of VO2max indicates greater aerobic endurance; well-trained individuals reach 70-90%. Conventional performance reference, not outcome-derived.
optimal.source: Faude O et al. 2009, Sports Med 39:469-490. PMID: 19453205

lactate_threshold.note: No outcome HRs in the heat map. No large general-population normative registry for lactate threshold; multiple non-interchangeable threshold definitions exist. Approximate.

grip_strength.population_source: Dodds RM et al. 2014, PLoS ONE, pooled British normative data (12 studies, ~49,964 individuals); both-sex midlife midpoint (men peak ~51 kg, women ~31 kg). PMID: 25474696

optimal.rationale: Mortality risk rises continuously with lower grip strength (HR ~1.27 per 5 kg lower); risk is minimized in the upper part of the distribution. Sex-specific sarcopenia cutpoints: <27 kg men, <16 kg women define low strength.
optimal.source: Leong DP et al. 2015 (PURE study, grip strength and mortality, PMID: 25982160); EWGSOP2 sarcopenia cutpoints, Cruz-Jentoft AJ et al. 2019, Age Ageing. PMID: 30312372

grip_strength.note: Strongly sex-dependent (men ~20 kg higher) and declines from ~age 50. Mean/SD shown is a both-sex midlife midpoint; use sex-specific norms for individual placement. Body-size adjustment also matters.

lower_body_strength.population_source: Relative sit-to-stand power ~2.5-3.5 W/kg in midlife adults. Alcazar J et al. 2018, Exp Gerontol, sit-to-stand power normative values; Alcazar J et al. 2020, J Cachexia Sarcopenia Muscle. PMID: 29452185

optimal.rationale: Higher relative lower-body power predicts lower mortality and fall/fracture risk; low relative power (below sex-specific cutpoints) is the risk category. Risk-direction reference from Alcazar normative thresholds.
optimal.source: Alcazar J et al. 2021, J Cachexia Sarcopenia Muscle, relative STS power and mortality. PMID: 33991414

lower_body_strength.note: Measured by varied methods (leg-press 1-RM, isokinetic, sit-to-stand power) with non-interchangeable units; sit-to-stand power (W/kg) used here. Strongly age/sex-dependent. Outcome HRs are categorical only. Approximate.

muscle_mass_almi.population_source: ALMI both-sex midlife midpoint (men ~8.5, women ~6.4 kg/m^2). NHANES DXA reference data, Kelly TL et al. 2009, PLoS ONE, US appendicular lean mass reference ranges. PMID: 19568432

optimal.rationale: Higher appendicular lean mass is protective (HR ~0.5 per 1-SD higher for mortality in prefrail/frail older adults). Sarcopenia cutpoints define the low-risk floor: <7.0 kg/m^2 men, <5.5 kg/m^2 women (EWGSOP2/DXA).
optimal.source: EWGSOP2, Cruz-Jentoft AJ et al. 2019, Age Ageing (PMID: 30312372); FNIH sarcopenia project, Studenski SA et al. 2014, J Gerontol. PMID: 24737557

muscle_mass_almi.note: Strongly sex-dependent (men ~2 kg/m^2 higher); mean/SD is a both-sex midlife midpoint, use sex-specific cutpoints for placement. BIA is a lower-accuracy surrogate for DXA.

gait_speed.population_source: Bohannon RW & Williams Andrews A 2011, Physiotherapy, meta-analysis of comfortable gait speed normative values across the lifespan; midlife adults ~1.3-1.4 m/s, ~1.2 representative for 60s. PMID: 21820535

optimal.rationale: Mortality falls with higher gait speed (HR ~0.71 per 0.1 m/s higher); risk minimized at >=1.2-1.4 m/s. ~0.8 m/s is a widely used disability/mortality risk threshold floor.
optimal.source: Studenski S et al. 2011, JAMA, pooled analysis of 34,485 adults, gait speed and survival. PMID: 21205966

gait_speed.note: Declines with age; mean/SD shown is a representative early-older-adult value. Use age/sex-specific norms for individual placement. Per-SD dementia HR uses 0.204 m/s as 1 SD.

sit_to_stand.population_source: Sitting-rising test (SRT) score distribution in adults 51-80: most score 8-10, mean ~8. Brito LB et al. 2014, Eur J Prev Cardiol, 2002 adults. PMID: 23242910

optimal.rationale: Lowest SRT score group (0-4) has markedly higher all-cause and cardiovascular mortality vs perfect score (10); risk minimized at scores 8-10.
optimal.source: Brito LB et al. 2014, Eur J Prev Cardiol 21:892-898, SRT score and mortality. PMID: 23242910; de Araujo CG et al. 2025 SRT mortality follow-up

sit_to_stand.note: Two distinct instruments with opposite directionality: SRT score (0-10, higher better, shown here) vs 5x sit-to-stand time in seconds (lower better). Outcome HRs are categorical only. Approximate.

vo2_kinetics.population_source: Phase II VO2 tau ~25-35 s in healthy middle-aged adults, slower (40-60+ s) in older/impaired adults. Murias JM et al. 2010-2014 work on VO2 kinetics and aging; representative value. DOI: 10.1249/MSS.0b013e3181c31f85

optimal.rationale: Faster VO2 kinetics (lower tau/MRT) reflect better muscle oxidative capacity and O2 delivery; trained/young individuals reach 15-25 s. Risk direction: slower kinetics predict poorer functional mobility.
optimal.source: Murias JM et al. 2010, J Appl Physiol, VO2 kinetics and training/age. DOI: 10.1152/japplphysiol.01040.2010

vo2_kinetics.note: No large general-population normative registry; mean/SD is a representative midlife value from kinetics literature. Outcome evidence (frailty) is associational only. Approximate.

heart_rate_recovery.population_source: 1-minute HRR after graded exercise ~25-30 bpm in general adults; abnormal defined as <=12 bpm (with active cool-down) or <=18 bpm. Cole CR et al. 1999, NEJM, 2428 adults. PMID: 10536127

optimal.rationale: Mortality risk rises with lower HRR (HR ~1.11 per 10 bpm lower); risk minimized with HRR well above the abnormal threshold. <=12-18 bpm marks the high-risk floor.
optimal.source: Cole CR et al. 1999, NEJM 341:1351-1357, abnormal HRR and mortality. PMID: 10536127

heart_rate_recovery.note: Depends on protocol (1- vs 2-minute, active vs passive cool-down); the 1-minute active-recovery value is shown. Influenced by peak HR achieved and fitness.

heart_rate_variability.population_source: Short-term (5-min) RMSSD normative values; median ~30-40 ms in midlife adults, right-skewed. Nunan D et al. 2010, Pacing Clin Electrophysiol, normative HRV review; Sammito S & Bockelmann I 2016, normative HRV reference values. PMID: 20663071

optimal.rationale: Mortality and CVD risk are concentrated in the lowest RMSSD/SDNN quartile (HR ~1.19-1.21); risk minimized in the upper half of the distribution. Higher vagally-mediated HRV is protective.
optimal.source: Jarczok MN et al. 2022, Neurosci Biobehav Rev, meta-analysis of HRV and all-cause mortality. PMID: 35870662

heart_rate_variability.note: RMSSD distribution is right-skewed and often analyzed log-transformed in research; heat-map HRs are quartile/median-split (per_sd basis listed but categorical), so a linear raw-ms scale is used here. Strongly age-dependent (falls with age), device- and posture-sensitive. Approximate.

resting_bp.population_source: NHANES 2017-2020 US adult mean systolic BP ~122-128 mmHg, SD ~16. Ostchega Y et al. / NHANES blood pressure normative data; NCHS Data Brief on hypertension prevalence. DOI: 10.15620/cdc:122044

optimal.rationale: Cardiovascular and all-cause mortality rise log-linearly from ~115 mmHg systolic with no clear lower threshold above ~90; SPRINT showed benefit targeting <120. Trough is the low-normal range.
optimal.source: Lewington S et al. 2002, Lancet, Prospective Studies Collaboration, 1 million adults, BP and vascular mortality (PMID: 12493255); SPRINT Research Group 2015, NEJM. PMID: 26551272

resting_bp.note: Systolic BP shown (most prognostic single component in midlife/older adults). Mildly U-shaped in frail older adults. Mean rises with age.

ambulatory_bp.population_source: IDACO (International Database on Ambulatory BP) population reference; 24-hour systolic mean ~118-121 mmHg. Thijs L et al. / Kikuya M et al. 2007, Hypertension, IDACO ambulatory BP reference values. PMID: 17576863

optimal.rationale: Mortality/CVD risk rises continuously with higher 24-h and nighttime systolic BP (HR ~1.23-1.45 per ~16 mmHg SD), independent of office BP; risk minimized in the low-normal range. ESH normalcy: 24-h <115-125 mmHg.
optimal.source: Yang WY et al. 2019, JAMA, IDACO, ambulatory BP and outcomes (PMID: 31479136); ESH 2023 ambulatory BP thresholds

ambulatory_bp.note: 24-hour systolic average shown; daytime, nighttime and dipping status are separate prognostic derivatives. Per-SD HRs use ~16 mmHg as 1 SD.

pulse_wave_velocity.population_source: Reference Values for Arterial Stiffness Collaboration 2010, Eur Heart J, 16,867 subjects; cf-PWV ~7-9 m/s in midlife adults, rising steeply with age and BP. PMID: 20631092

optimal.rationale: All-cause mortality and CVD risk rise continuously with higher cf-PWV (HR ~1.52 per 1 m/s; ~1.3 per SD log-PWV); >10 m/s is the established high-risk threshold. Lower stiffness is risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: Vlachopoulos C et al. 2010, J Am Coll Cardiol, meta-analysis of PWV and outcomes (PMID: 20338492); ESC/ESH 10 m/s threshold

pulse_wave_velocity.note: cf-PWV rises steeply with age and blood pressure; mean/SD shown is a representative midlife value (use age-stratified reference table for placement). One CVD outcome uses log-transformed PWV per SD, but the marker is conventionally reported and interpreted on the linear m/s scale.

endothelial_function.population_source: Brachial FMD ~5-8% in healthy midlife adults, declining with age. Skaug EA et al. 2013, Eur Heart J, HUNT3 study, FMD reference values in 4739 adults. PMID: 23381959

optimal.rationale: Higher FMD predicts lower CVD events (HR ~0.73 per 1% higher) and lower dementia risk; risk minimized in the upper part of the distribution. No outcome-defined cutpoint, so an upper-range reference is given.
optimal.source: Inaba Y et al. 2010, Int J Cardiovasc Imaging, meta-analysis of FMD and cardiovascular events. PMID: 20857234

endothelial_function.note: FMD is highly operator- and protocol-dependent with substantial between-lab variability; mean/SD is a representative healthy-adult value. PAT/reactive hyperemia index is a separate, non-interchangeable measure. Approximate.

resting_heart_rate.population_source: NHANES US adult resting heart rate distribution, mean ~71 bpm, SD ~11. Ostchega Y et al. 2011, Natl Health Stat Report, resting pulse rate in US adults. PMID: 21905522

optimal.rationale: All-cause, CVD and cancer mortality rise with higher RHR (HR ~1.10-1.17 per 10 bpm); risk minimized roughly 50-65 bpm. Very low RHR (<40) in non-athletes can reflect conduction disease, hence not lower-is-always-better.
optimal.source: Zhang D et al. 2016, CMAJ, dose-response meta-analysis of RHR and all-cause/CVD mortality. PMID: 27551033

resting_heart_rate.note: Mildly U-shaped at extreme low values in non-athletes; trained endurance athletes have low RHR without elevated risk. Wearable overnight RHR runs lower than seated clinic pulse.

echo_measures.units: g/m^2 (LV mass index) or unitless (E/e' ratio)
echo_measures.population_source: LV mass index (echo, indexed to BSA) both-sex midpoint ~75-85 g/m^2 (men ~85, women ~75). ASE/EACVI Chamber Quantification reference ranges, Lang RM et al. 2015, J Am Soc Echocardiogr. PMID: 25559473

optimal.rationale: All-cause mortality and CVD events rise continuously with higher LV mass per SD; LVH is defined as >95 g/m^2 (women) / >115 g/m^2 (men). Lower-normal LV mass and normal diastolic function are risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: Lang RM et al. 2015, ASE/EACVI Chamber Quantification (LVH thresholds, PMID: 25559473); Levy D et al. 1990, NEJM, Framingham LV mass and mortality. PMID: 2139921

echo_measures.note: Composite domain: LV mass index (linear, shown here) plus diastolic-function indices (E/e', e', LA volume index) which are separate measures. Sex-specific LVH cutpoints differ. Treat the single LV-mass scale as approximate for the broader echo domain.

processing_speed.population_source: By construction: cognitive processing speed in the heat map is reported as a within-cohort standardized z-score (e.g., DSST/SDMT/reaction-time scores normed to mean 0, SD 1). No external population concentration units apply.

optimal.rationale: Risk of dementia and mortality rises ~1.25-1.65 per 1-SD lower score; risk minimized at or above the cohort mean. Expressed on the z-scale because the underlying tests are heterogeneous and age-normed.
optimal.source: Hooper J et al. / Kuh D et al.; Welmer AK et al. processing speed and outcomes; conventional cognitive-aging z-score framing

processing_speed.note: Not a single quantitative measurement: processing speed is captured by multiple non-interchangeable tests (DSST, SDMT, Trail A, reaction time) and reported only as scaled/normed z-scores. Population mean 0 / SD 1 holds by construction, not from an external concentration source. applicable=false.

working_memory.population_source: By construction: working memory in the heat map is reported as a within-cohort standardized z-score (digit span backward, n-back, NIH Toolbox List Sorting normed to mean 0, SD 1). No external population concentration units apply.

optimal.rationale: Dementia/frailty/mortality risk rises per 1-SD lower score; risk minimized at or above the cohort mean. Expressed on the z-scale because underlying tasks are heterogeneous.
optimal.source: Conventional cognitive-aging z-score framing; per-SD HRs in heat map outcomes (working-memory/executive composite)

working_memory.note: Not a single quantitative measurement: a battery of non-interchangeable tasks reported only as normed z-scores. Population mean 0 / SD 1 holds by construction. applicable=false.

executive_function.population_source: By construction: executive function in the heat map is reported as a within-cohort standardized composite z-score (Trail B, Stroop, fluency, NIH Toolbox Flanker normed to mean 0, SD 1). No external population concentration units apply.

optimal.rationale: Incident dementia risk rises ~1.6 per 1-SD lower composite score; CHD risk elevated in lowest tertile. Risk minimized at or above the cohort mean.
optimal.source: Conventional cognitive-aging z-score framing; per-SD/tertile HRs in heat map outcomes

executive_function.note: Not a single quantitative measurement: a composite of heterogeneous tests reported only as normed z-scores. Population mean 0 / SD 1 holds by construction. applicable=false.

ptau217.population_source: Cognitively unimpaired older adults: plasma p-tau217 (ALZpath Simoa) geometric mean ~0.4-0.5 pg/mL; ln-mean ~-0.8, ln-SD ~0.6 (markedly right-skewed). Ashton NJ et al. 2024, JAMA Neurol, ALZpath p-tau217 across cohorts. PMID: 38165442

optimal.rationale: Lower is better; risk of progression to Alzheimer dementia rises ~3.2 per 1-SD higher log p-tau217. Assay-specific clinical cutpoints (~0.4 pg/mL ALZpath) define abnormality; values well below the cohort mean are risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: Ashton NJ et al. 2024, JAMA Neurol 81:255-263 (PMID: 38165442); Mattsson-Carlgren N et al. p-tau217 and progression

ptau217.note: Strongly assay-specific (Lilly/MSD, ALZpath, Janssen, %p-tau217 ratio variants not interchangeable); ln-scale mean/SD shown are approximate for the ALZpath Simoa assay in cognitively unimpaired adults. Rises with age and amyloid status. Confirm assay-specific reference before clinical use.

nfl.population_source: Plasma NfL (Simoa) rises steeply with age; midlife-adult geometric mean ~13-16 pg/mL (ln-mean ~2.7, ln-SD ~0.45-0.5). Simren J et al. 2022, Brain Commun, age-stratified NfL reference percentiles (>5000 individuals). PMID: 35813882

optimal.rationale: Lower is better; all-cause mortality (HR ~1.75/SD), incident dementia (~1.92/SD) and stroke rise with higher log NfL. No outcome-defined cutpoint; values below the age-specific mean are risk-minimizing. Use age-adjusted percentiles (e.g., <95th).
optimal.source: Simren J et al. 2022, Brain Commun 4:fcac174 (PMID: 35813882); Kaeser SA et al. age-adjusted NfL reference

nfl.note: NfL rises markedly with age and is affected by renal function and BMI; ln-scale mean/SD shown is a representative midlife value, but individual placement must use age-stratified percentiles. Simoa platform-specific. Approximate.

gfap.population_source: Plasma GFAP (Simoa) rises with age; midlife/older-adult geometric mean ~100-130 pg/mL (ln-mean ~4.6-4.8, ln-SD ~0.45-0.5). Pichet Binette A et al. 2024 / Simoa GFAP age-stratified reference data. DOI: 10.1038/s41591-024-02857-3

optimal.rationale: Lower is better; incident Alzheimer/related dementia risk rises ~1.72 per 1-SD higher log GFAP, with associations also for mortality and CVD. No outcome-defined cutpoint; values below the age-specific mean are risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: Pichet Binette A et al. 2024, Nat Med, plasma GFAP and AD progression. DOI: 10.1038/s41591-024-02857-3

gfap.note: GFAP rises with age and is influenced by renal function and BMI; ln-scale mean/SD is a representative value, individual placement should use age-stratified percentiles. Simoa platform-specific and assay-dependent. Approximate.

hscrp.population_source: Derived from NHANES 1999-2010 hsCRP distributions; geometric mean ~1.6-1.8 mg/L, broadly right-skewed (ln-scale). Consistent with Woloshin & Schwartz 2005, Arch Intern Med (PMID:16157830) and NHANES summary statistics in Ridker 2003 Circulation (PMID:12551853).

optimal.rationale: CRP <1 mg/L defines low cardiovascular risk (AHA/CDC); mortality and CVD HRs rise log-linearly across the range, so the lowest measurable values are risk-minimizing. Above ~10 mg/L reflects acute infection and should be re-tested.
optimal.source: Pearson TA et al. AHA/CDC scientific statement. Circulation 2003;107:499-511. PMID:12551879

hscrp.note: Right-skewed; analyzed log-transformed (per_log_sd in heat map). ln-scale mean/SD approximate (geometric mean ~1.67 mg/L). Values >10 mg/L usually indicate acute inflammation, not chronic risk.

il6.population_source: General-population IL-6 distributions; geometric mean ~1.5-1.6 pg/mL with strong right skew. Consistent with Cardiovascular Health Study and Health ABC cohorts (Harris TB et al. Am J Med 1999, PMID:10428105) and IL6R Mendelian-randomization consortium descriptive data.

optimal.rationale: Risk rises log-linearly with IL-6; lowest values are optimal. <1.5-2 pg/mL associated with lowest mortality and CVD risk in older-adult cohorts.
optimal.source: IL6R Genetics Consortium / Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration. Lancet 2012;379:1205-1213. PMID:22421339

il6.note: Right-skewed; analyzed log-transformed (per_log_sd). ln-scale mean/SD approximate. Assay variability between platforms is substantial.

tnf_alpha.population_source: Framingham Offspring and Health ABC cohort TNF-alpha distributions; geometric mean ~2.5 pg/mL. Consistent with Cesari M et al. Circulation 2003 (PMID:12515748) and Health ABC inflammatory marker descriptives.

optimal.rationale: Higher TNF-alpha tracks with mortality and frailty (top vs bottom tertile HR ~1.2-1.5); lowest tertile is risk-minimizing. No formal outcome-derived cutpoint, so conventional low-tertile range used.
optimal.source: Cesari M et al. Inflammatory markers and onset of cardiovascular events. Circulation 2003;108:2317-2322. PMID:14568895

tnf_alpha.note: Right-skewed; conventionally log-analyzed and heat map gives a per_sd HR alongside tertiles. ln-scale mean/SD approximate; soluble TNF receptors (sTNFR1/2) are more stable analytes.

glyca.population_source: NMR-measured GlycA in PREVEND and Women's Health Study populations; mean ~1.3-1.4 mmol/L. Consistent with Akinkuolie AO et al. Arterioscler Thromb Vasc Biol 2014 (PMID:24895013) and Duprez DA et al. (MESA).

optimal.rationale: GlycA is roughly normally distributed and analyzed per_sd; risk rises monotonically, so the lower part of the distribution (bottom quartile, ~<1.3 mmol/L) is risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: Akinkuolie AO et al. A novel protein glycan biomarker and future cardiovascular disease events. Arterioscler Thromb Vasc Biol 2014;34:1922-1928. PMID:24895013

glyca.note: NMR composite glycoprotein signal; only roughly normal so linear scale used (per_sd in heat map). Platform-specific (LabCorp/Nightingale) calibration differs.

nlr.population_source: NHANES 1999-2010 derived neutrophil:lymphocyte ratio in healthy adults; mean ~2.0-2.2, SD ~1.0. Consistent with Forget P et al. BMC Res Notes 2017 (PMID:28057051) reference-value study and NHANES-based analyses (Song M et al.).

optimal.rationale: NLR ~1-2 is the conventional healthy range and associated with lowest mortality; risk rises above ~3. Values <1 may reflect lymphocytosis. Lower-better within the normal band.
optimal.source: Forget P et al. What is the normal value of the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio? BMC Res Notes 2017;10:12. PMID:28057051

nlr.note: Derived ratio analyzed per_sd; mildly right-skewed but linear scale used to match heat-map metric. Sensitive to acute illness, stress, and corticosteroids.

fibrinogen.population_source: Fibrinogen Studies Collaboration individual-participant meta-analysis (154,000+ adults); usual mean ~3.0 g/L, SD ~0.6. Danesh J et al. JAMA 2005;294:1799-1809. PMID:16219884.

optimal.rationale: Risk rises approximately log-linearly with fibrinogen; the Fibrinogen Studies Collaboration showed continuous mortality/CVD gradients, so the lower part of the normal range (~2-3 g/L) is risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: Fibrinogen Studies Collaboration (Danesh J et al.). Plasma fibrinogen level and risk of major cardiovascular disease and nonvascular mortality. JAMA 2005;294:1799-1809. PMID:16219884

fibrinogen.note: Heat map uses per_sd; distribution is only modestly skewed so linear scale used. Acute-phase reactant - elevated transiently with infection/inflammation.

homocysteine.population_source: NHANES 1999-2004 plasma total homocysteine in US adults; geometric mean ~8-9 umol/L, arithmetic mean ~9-10 umol/L. Consistent with Ganji V & Kafai MR, Am J Clin Nutr 2003 (PMID:12816782).

optimal.rationale: Homocysteine <10 umol/L is associated with lowest mortality, CVD and dementia risk; risk rises continuously above this. Hyperhomocysteinemia >15 umol/L is the conventional clinical threshold.
optimal.source: Homocysteine Studies Collaboration. Homocysteine and risk of ischemic heart disease and stroke. JAMA 2002;288:2015-2022. PMID:12387654

homocysteine.note: Right-skewed but heat map outcomes use per_unit (linear); linear scale used. Values rise with B12/folate deficiency, renal impairment, and delayed sample processing.

ferritin.population_source: NHANES serum ferritin distributions; strongly right-skewed, geometric mean ~50-60 ng/mL overall (markedly sex-dependent: men ~120, premenopausal women ~30). Consistent with NHANES iron-status reports (Cogswell ME et al. Am J Clin Nutr 2009, PMID:19357218).

optimal.rationale: As an inflammation/iron-overload marker, both extremes carry risk: <30 indicates iron deficiency, >150-300 associates with hepatic and metabolic risk and reflects inflammation. Mid-range is risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: WHO. Serum ferritin concentrations for the assessment of iron status. WHO/NMH/NHD/MNM/11.2, 2020; and Camaschella C. NEJM 2015;372:1832. PMID:25946282

ferritin.note: Strongly right-skewed; log scale used (heat map uses quartiles for an inflammation framing). Acute-phase reactant - elevated by infection/inflammation independent of iron stores. Strongly sex- and age-dependent.

supar.population_source: General-population suPAR distributions from the Danish MONICA10 / Inter99 cohorts and Malmo Diet & Cancer; median ~2.5-3.0 ng/mL, right-skewed. Eugen-Olsen J et al. J Intern Med 2010 (PMID:20561148).

optimal.rationale: Risk rises continuously with suPAR; values <3 ng/mL are associated with lowest mortality and CKD risk in population cohorts. Lower-better.
optimal.source: Eugen-Olsen J et al. Circulating soluble urokinase plasminogen activator receptor predicts cancer, cardiovascular disease, diabetes and mortality. J Intern Med 2010;268:296-308. PMID:20561148

supar.note: Right-skewed; conventionally log-analyzed (heat map gives per_sd). ln-scale mean/SD approximate (geometric mean ~2.6 ng/mL). Less affected by acute inflammation than CRP.

egfr_cystatin_c.population_source: NHANES 1999-2018 cystatin C-based eGFR (CKD-EPI 2012/2021) in US adults; mean ~85-95, declines with age. Consistent with Inker LA et al. NEJM 2012 (PMID:22762315) and NHANES CKD surveillance reports.

optimal.rationale: Mortality is lowest at eGFR ~90-105; risk rises steeply below 60 and modestly at very high values. Higher-better within the normal-to-high range. Per_sd lower eGFR = higher risk.
optimal.source: Shlipak MG et al. Cystatin C versus creatinine in determining risk based on kidney function. NEJM 2013;369:932-943. PMID:24004120

egfr_cystatin_c.note: Linear scale (heat map per_sd). Strongly age-dependent; a mild U-shape exists at very high eGFR but lower values dominate risk. Cystatin C-based eGFR less affected by muscle mass than creatinine-based.

cystatin_c.population_source: NHANES 1999-2002 serum cystatin C in US adults; mean ~0.85-0.95 mg/L, rises with age. Kottgen A et al. Am J Kidney Dis 2008 (PMID:18495303); standardized to ERM-DA471 reference material.

optimal.rationale: Lower cystatin C reflects better kidney function and is associated with lower mortality and CVD risk; risk rises continuously above ~1.0 mg/L. Lower-better.
optimal.source: Shlipak MG et al. Cystatin C and the risk of death and cardiovascular events among elderly persons. NEJM 2005;352:2049-2060. PMID:15901858

cystatin_c.note: Linear scale (heat map per_sd). Age-dependent; also elevated by inflammation, thyroid dysfunction, and corticosteroids independent of GFR.

albuminuria_uacr.population_source: NHANES urine albumin:creatinine ratio in US adults; strongly right-skewed, median ~7-8 mg/g, geometric mean ~7 mg/g. Consistent with NHANES CKD surveillance and Chronic Kidney Disease Prognosis Consortium descriptive data.

optimal.rationale: Mortality and CVD/CKD risk rise continuously from very low UACR; values <10 mg/g (well below the 30 mg/g microalbuminuria threshold) are risk-minimizing. Lower-better.
optimal.source: Chronic Kidney Disease Prognosis Consortium (Matsushita K et al.). Lancet 2010;375:2073-2081. PMID:20483451

albuminuria_uacr.note: Strongly right-skewed; log scale used (heat map per_log_sd / per_doubling). ln-scale mean/SD approximate. High day-to-day variability; spot UACR should be confirmed.

alt.population_source: NHANES serum ALT distributions in US adults; right-skewed, median ~20-22 U/L (men higher than women). Consistent with Ruhl CE & Everhart JE, Gastroenterology 2012 (PMID:22326434).

optimal.rationale: A J-shaped mortality curve exists - very low ALT signals frailty/sarcopenia in older adults, very high signals liver injury. The risk trough is ~17-25 U/L; updated healthy thresholds are <25 (women) / <33 (men).
optimal.source: Kunutsor SK et al. Liver aminotransferases and risk of all-cause mortality: a meta-analysis. Am J Epidemiol 2014;179:1158-1170. PMID:24682939

alt.note: Right-skewed; log scale used (heat map per_sd applied on skewed analyte, conventionally log-analyzed). U-shaped mortality: low ALT associated with frailty. Sex-dependent.

ast.population_source: NHANES serum AST distributions in US adults; right-skewed, median ~21-23 U/L. Consistent with Ruhl CE & Everhart JE, Gastroenterology 2012 (PMID:22326434).

optimal.rationale: Mortality rises at both low (frailty) and high (liver/cardiac injury) AST; the trough is ~20-30 U/L. AST/ALT ratio adds prognostic information.
optimal.source: Kunutsor SK et al. Aspartate aminotransferase and risk of mortality: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Maturitas 2015;81:351-358. PMID:25998690

ast.note: Right-skewed; log scale used. AST is less liver-specific than ALT (also released from muscle, heart, RBCs).

ggt.population_source: NHANES serum GGT distributions in US adults; strongly right-skewed, median ~18-22 U/L (men higher). Consistent with Ruhl CE & Everhart JE, Hepatology 2009 (PMID:19582814).

optimal.rationale: GGT shows a continuous log-linear association with mortality, CVD and cancer; the lowest tertile/decile is risk-minimizing. Low-normal GGT (~<25 U/L) is optimal.
optimal.source: Kunutsor SK et al. Gamma-glutamyltransferase and risk of all-cause, cardiovascular and liver mortality. Eur J Epidemiol 2017;32:289-308. PMID:28425046

ggt.note: Strongly right-skewed; log scale used (heat map per_log_sd). ln-scale mean/SD approximate. Sensitive marker of alcohol intake, hepatic steatosis, and oxidative stress.

fib4.population_source: NHANES-derived FIB-4 distributions in US adults; mean ~1.0-1.2, rises with age. Consistent with Vilar-Gomez E et al. and NHANES-based FIB-4 prevalence studies (Kim D et al. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol).

optimal.rationale: FIB-4 <1.3 rules out advanced fibrosis and is associated with lowest mortality; 1.3-2.67 is indeterminate; >2.67 indicates advanced fibrosis. Age-adjusted cutoff 2.0 used over age 65.
optimal.source: Sterling RK et al. Development of a simple noninvasive index to predict significant fibrosis in HIV/HCV coinfection. Hepatology 2006;43:1317-1325. PMID:16729309

fib4.note: Calculated index from age, AST, ALT, platelets; not a single measured analyte but a continuous quantitative score, so scorable. Heat map uses categorical (cutpoint) HRs. Age strongly inflates FIB-4.

uric_acid.population_source: NHANES 2015-2016 serum uric acid in US adults; mean ~5.4 mg/dL (men ~6.0, women ~4.8). Consistent with NHANES gout/hyperuricemia surveillance (Chen-Xu M et al. Arthritis Rheumatol 2019, PMID:30618180).

optimal.rationale: U-shaped mortality: low uric acid associates with malnutrition/frailty and loss of antioxidant capacity, high uric acid with gout, CKD and CVD. The risk trough is ~4-5.5 mg/dL.
optimal.source: Cho SK et al. U-shaped association between serum uric acid level and risk of mortality in U.S. adults. Arthritis Rheumatol 2018;70:1122-1132. PMID:29569437

uric_acid.note: Linear scale (heat map per_sd). U-shaped mortality association well documented. Sex-dependent (men higher). Elevated by renal impairment, diuretics, and high-purine/fructose intake.

vitamin_d_25oh.population_source: NHANES 2011-2014 standardized serum 25(OH)D in US adults; mean ~25-26 ng/mL (62-65 nmol/L). Herrick KA et al. Am J Clin Nutr 2019 (PMID:31022277).

optimal.rationale: U-shaped mortality: risk is elevated below ~20 ng/mL and a possible slight uptick above ~60-100 ng/mL. The risk-minimizing trough is ~30-50 ng/mL (75-125 nmol/L).
optimal.source: Durazo-Arvizu RA et al. (D-CarDia) and Gaksch M et al. Vitamin D and mortality: individual participant data meta-analysis. PLoS One 2017;12:e0170791. PMID:28207791

vitamin_d_25oh.note: Linear scale (heat map per_sd). Strong seasonal and latitude variation. Mortality association is partly confounded by reverse causation (illness lowers 25(OH)D).

vitamin_b12.population_source: NHANES serum vitamin B12 in US adults; right-skewed, geometric mean ~450-500 pg/mL. Consistent with Pfeiffer CM et al. Am J Clin Nutr 2013 NHANES B-vitamin status report (PMID:23596162).

optimal.rationale: U-shaped mortality: deficiency (<200-300 pg/mL) causes anemia/neuropathy; very high B12 (>800-1000 pg/mL) associates with higher mortality, often as a marker of liver disease or malignancy. Mid-normal range is optimal.
optimal.source: Flores-Guerrero JL et al. Association of plasma concentration of vitamin B12 with all-cause mortality. JAMA Netw Open 2020;3:e1919274. PMID:31922561

vitamin_b12.note: Right-skewed; log scale used. Total B12 is an imperfect status marker - MMA and holotranscobalamin are more sensitive. Heat map outcomes are categorical.

folate.population_source: NHANES post-fortification serum folate in US adults; mean ~15-17 ng/mL. Pfeiffer CM et al. Am J Clin Nutr 2012 (PMID:22301929).

optimal.rationale: Adequate serum folate (>4-5 ng/mL) prevents megaloblastic anemia and elevated homocysteine; mortality is lowest in the mid-range. Very high serum folate may relate to unmetabolized folic acid; conventional adequacy range used.
optimal.source: Bailey RL et al. and IOM Dietary Reference Intakes for folate; NHANES folate status (Pfeiffer CM et al. PMID:22301929)

folate.note: Heat map gives per_sd alongside quartiles. Post-fortification US population has high folate; deficiency is rare. RBC folate is a better long-term status marker than serum.

methylmalonic_acid.population_source: NHANES serum methylmalonic acid in US adults; strongly right-skewed, geometric mean ~0.13-0.15 umol/L, rises with age and renal impairment. Pfeiffer CM et al. Am J Clin Nutr 2013 (PMID:23596162).

optimal.rationale: MMA <0.27 umol/L is the conventional threshold for adequate B12 status; elevated MMA marks functional B12 deficiency and tracks with mortality and cognitive decline. Lower-better.
optimal.source: Pfeiffer CM et al. Applying inappropriate cutoffs leads to misinterpretation of folate status. Am J Clin Nutr 2013;98:1297-1300; and NHANES B12 status reports

methylmalonic_acid.note: Strongly right-skewed; log scale used (population_mean/SD are ln of umol/L). Elevated by renal impairment independent of B12 status. Heat map outcomes categorical.

omega3_index.population_source: US general-population RBC omega-3 index (EPA+DHA); mean ~4-5%, low relative to cardioprotective targets. Stark KD et al. Prog Lipid Res 2016 global survey (PMID:26920564); Harris WS et al.

optimal.rationale: An omega-3 index >=8% is associated with the lowest total mortality and CVD risk; 4-8% intermediate, <4% highest risk. Higher-better up to ~8-12%.
optimal.source: Harris WS et al. Erythrocyte omega-3 index, mortality, and risk of incident CVD: a pooled analysis. Am J Clin Nutr 2021;113:1447-1454. PMID:33564854

omega3_index.note: Linear scale (heat map quartiles/quintiles). Strongly influenced by fish/supplement intake. Some plateau above ~8% so functionally higher-better with a target band.

magnesium_rbc.population_source: RBC magnesium reference distributions from clinical laboratory populations; typical mean ~5.0 mg/dL (range ~4.2-6.8 mg/dL). No large NHANES RBC-Mg dataset exists (NHANES measures serum Mg).

optimal.rationale: RBC magnesium reflects intracellular stores better than serum; mid-to-upper reference range is associated with lower arrhythmia and metabolic risk. Conventional reference range used (no outcome-derived cutpoint).
optimal.source: Conventional clinical-laboratory reference interval (e.g., Mayo/Quest RBC magnesium); no large-cohort outcome data

magnesium_rbc.note: No large general-population RBC-magnesium dataset; population_mean/SD are typical lab reference values, not a cited cohort statistic - verification_status unverified. Serum magnesium has far more outcome data.

zinc.population_source: NHANES II serum zinc in US adults; mean ~85-90 ug/dL. Hotz C et al. (NHANES II zinc reference) Am J Clin Nutr 2003 (PMID:12816771); IZiNCG reference values.

optimal.rationale: Conventional adequacy range; both deficiency (immune dysfunction, poor wound healing) and excess (copper depletion) carry risk. Mid-reference range is optimal. No outcome-derived cutpoint, so reference interval used.
optimal.source: International Zinc Nutrition Consultative Group (IZiNCG) reference values; Hotz C et al. PMID:12816771

zinc.note: Linear scale. Serum zinc is an insensitive status marker (homeostatically buffered); falls during acute inflammation. Diurnal variation and fasting state affect values. Heat map has no usable per-SD HR.

copper.population_source: NHANES 2011-2016 serum copper in US adults; mean ~100-115 ug/dL (women higher; raised by estrogen/oral contraceptives). Consistent with NHANES trace-element reports.

optimal.rationale: U-shaped: deficiency impairs antioxidant and connective-tissue function; high serum copper (top tertile/quartile) associates with elevated all-cause, CVD and cancer mortality, partly as an inflammation marker. Mid-low normal is risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: Malavolta M et al. Serum copper to zinc ratio and mortality in the elderly. and Bo S et al. associations of serum copper with mortality. (NHANES-based copper-mortality analyses, e.g. PMID:35613835)

copper.note: Linear scale. Serum copper rises with inflammation (ceruloplasmin is an acute-phase protein) and estrogen; high values partly reflect inflammation rather than copper excess. Sex-dependent.

ceruloplasmin.population_source: Clinical-laboratory reference distributions for serum ceruloplasmin in adults; typical mean ~28-32 mg/dL (range ~20-40 mg/dL). No large NHANES ceruloplasmin dataset; raised by estrogen and inflammation.

optimal.rationale: Conventional reference range; low ceruloplasmin suggests Wilson disease/copper deficiency, high values reflect acute-phase inflammation and associate with higher CVD/mortality risk in quartile analyses. Mid-reference range used.
optimal.source: Conventional clinical-laboratory reference interval; quartile mortality associations from inflammation cohorts (no single cited cohort statistic)

ceruloplasmin.note: No large general-population cohort statistic available; population_mean/SD are typical lab reference values - verification_status unverified. Acute-phase reactant strongly raised by inflammation and estrogen.

transferrin_saturation.population_source: NHANES serum transferrin saturation in US adults; mean ~26-28%. Consistent with NHANES iron-status reports (Cogswell ME et al. Am J Clin Nutr 2009, PMID:19357218).

optimal.rationale: U-shaped mortality: low TSAT (<20%) indicates iron deficiency, high TSAT (>45-50%) indicates iron overload/hemochromatosis and tracks with higher mortality. The risk trough is ~25-35%.
optimal.source: Ellervik C et al. and Pfeiffenberger J; UK Biobank transferrin saturation-mortality analyses (e.g. Daghlas I et al., PMID:33500563)

transferrin_saturation.note: Linear scale. Diurnal variation (higher in morning) and affected by fasting state and inflammation. Heat map outcomes categorical.

tibc.population_source: NHANES total iron-binding capacity in US adults; mean ~340-360 ug/dL. Consistent with NHANES iron-status reports (Cogswell ME et al. Am J Clin Nutr 2009, PMID:19357218).

optimal.rationale: TIBC sits within the conventional reference range; high TIBC reflects iron deficiency, low TIBC reflects iron overload, inflammation or malnutrition. Mid-reference range is optimal. No outcome-derived cutpoint.
optimal.source: Conventional clinical-laboratory reference interval; NHANES iron-status descriptive data (PMID:19357218)

tibc.note: Linear scale. TIBC is largely a proxy for transferrin; interpreted jointly with serum iron and ferritin. Low TIBC also a malnutrition/inflammation marker. Heat map uses quartiles.

selenium.population_source: NHANES 2011-2016 serum selenium in US adults; mean ~120-128 ug/L (US population is selenium-replete). Consistent with NHANES trace-element reports and Bleys J et al. Arch Intern Med 2008 (PMID:18299496).

optimal.rationale: U-shaped mortality: risk falls as selenium rises to ~120-150 ug/L (optimizing selenoprotein/glutathione-peroxidase activity) then rises again above ~150-160 ug/L (pro-diabetic/pro-mortality). The trough is ~120-150 ug/L.
optimal.source: Bleys J et al. Serum selenium levels and all-cause, cancer, and cardiovascular mortality among US adults. Arch Intern Med 2008;168:404-410. PMID:18299496

selenium.note: Linear scale (heat map per_sd/quartile). Strongly geography-dependent (soil selenium); US population is replete, so a higher trough applies than in deficient regions.

tmao_choline.population_source: Plasma TMAO distributions from the Cleveland Clinic GeneBank cohort and PREDIMED; strongly right-skewed, median ~3-4 umol/L. Tang WHW et al. NEJM 2013 (PMID:23614584).

optimal.rationale: Risk rises continuously with TMAO; the lowest quartile (~<5 umol/L) is associated with lowest MACE and mortality. Lower-better.
optimal.source: Tang WHW et al. Intestinal microbial metabolism of phosphatidylcholine and cardiovascular risk. NEJM 2013;368:1575-1584. PMID:23614584; meta-analysis Heianza Y et al. J Am Heart Assoc 2017 PMID:28663251

tmao_choline.note: Strongly right-skewed; log scale used (population_mean/SD are ln of umol/L, approximate). Highly diet- and microbiome-dependent and elevated by renal impairment. Heat map outcomes categorical.

cortisol_morning_serum.population_source: Approximate from morning (08:00-09:00) serum cortisol distributions; representative of healthy adults, e.g. Reynolds 2010 / population HPA studies. No single NHANES serum-cortisol release covers all years; value approximate.

optimal.rationale: Mid-range morning cortisol; both very low (adrenal insufficiency) and very high (chronic HPA activation, adverse metabolic/CVD/mortality outcomes) carry risk. Conventional morning reference ~140-550 nmol/L; risk-minimizing band is the middle of that range.
optimal.source: Conventional clinical reference range; Kumari 2011 PMID:21307138 (flat/high cortisol exposure and mortality)

cortisol_morning_serum.note: Assumes a single standardized 07:00-09:00 venous draw. Strong diurnal/pulsatile variation makes a single value imprecise. Sexes broadly similar for total serum cortisol; oral estrogen/pregnancy raise CBG and total cortisol. Mean/SD approximate, not from a single definitive general-population PMID.

cortisol_diurnal_slope.units: log10(nmol/L) per hour (slope of log-cortisol vs time since wake)
cortisol_diurnal_slope.population_source: Approximate from pooled salivary diurnal-slope literature (e.g. Adam 2017 meta-analysis of diurnal cortisol slope and health outcomes, PMID:28892905).

optimal.rationale: A steeper (more negative) decline from morning peak to evening trough is healthy; flatter (less negative, toward 0) slopes index HPA dysregulation and predict higher mortality, CVD and inflammation.

cortisol_diurnal_slope.note: Slope is a derived quantity; units and computation vary across studies (log10/hr vs log/hr; with or without the cortisol awakening response). 'lower_better' means more negative slope is healthier. Mean/SD approximate; depends heavily on sampling protocol.

cortisol_hair.population_source: Natural-log of hair cortisol concentration; approximate from population HCC distributions (median ~5-8 pg/mg, strongly right-skewed), e.g. Stalder 2017 meta-analysis PMID:28135674.

optimal.rationale: Mid-range chronic cortisol exposure (raw pg/mg). Elevated HCC indexes chronic stress load and associates with cardiometabolic risk; conventional range only, no validated mortality-trough.
optimal.source: Stalder 2017 PMID:28135674 (conventional/normative reference)

cortisol_hair.note: HCC is strongly right-skewed so analyzed on log scale; population_mean/sd are of the NATURAL LOG of pg/mg. Optimal low/high given in raw pg/mg. Assays not standardized; hair treatment/washing affect values. No definitive single PMID for population mean/SD.

dheas.population_source: Approximate adult-population serum DHEA-S; declines steeply with age (adrenopause). Representative of mid-adult range; NHANES did not routinely assay DHEA-S. Sex- and age-specific norms essential.

optimal.rationale: Higher DHEA-S within the youthful-adult range is associated with lower frailty and mortality; very low DHEA-S marks biological aging. Direction is broadly higher-better within physiological range.
optimal.source: Conventional reference; Ohlsson 2010 PMID:20650977 (low DHEA-S and mortality in older men)

dheas.note: DHEA-S falls ~80% between age 25 and 75, so a single population mean is age-dependent; value approximates a mid-adult (~40-50y) reference. Reference sex: men (women run somewhat lower). Use age-specific norms in practice. Mean/SD approximate.

optimal.rationale: No standardized reference range exists; units depend on which units each analyte is expressed in. A rising ratio indexes catabolic shift with age but lacks a validated population distribution or risk-optimal band.

cortisol_dheas_ratio.note: Derived ratio with arbitrary, non-standardized units; outcomes use categorical (tertile) metrics only. No general-population mean/SD is sourceable, so applicable:false. Interpret only relative to a within-study reference. A higher ratio = catabolic dominance is generally adverse.

tsh.population_source: Natural-log of serum TSH; NHANES III disease-free reference population, geometric mean ~1.5 mIU/L (ln ~0.42). Hollowell 2002 J Clin Endocrinol Metab PMID:11836274.

optimal.rationale: U-shaped mortality association: both subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH) and subclinical hypothyroidism (high TSH) raise CVD/mortality risk. Lowest-risk trough lies in the lower-mid reference range (~0.5-2.5 mIU/L); modestly higher TSH (up to ~4) may be benign in the elderly.
optimal.source: Hollowell 2002 PMID:11836274; Asvold 2017 / thyroid-mortality literature

tsh.note: TSH is log-normally distributed; population_mean/sd are of the NATURAL LOG of mIU/L (geometric mean ~1.5). Optimal low/high in raw mIU/L. Distribution shifts upward with age; sexes broadly similar.

free_t4.population_source: Approximate adult-population free T4 (NHANES / large cohorts), reference range ~10-23 pmol/L (0.8-1.8 ng/dL). Hollowell 2002 PMID:11836274.

optimal.rationale: Within-reference-range higher free T4 is associated with greater all-cause mortality, atrial fibrillation and bone loss; lower-normal free T4 is metabolically favorable. Risk-minimizing band is the lower-mid reference range.
optimal.source: Hollowell 2002 PMID:11836274; Yeap 2012 / free T4-mortality cohort studies

free_t4.note: Free T4 estimates are method-dependent; reference ranges are assay-specific. Mean/SD approximate, in pmol/L (divide by ~12.87 for ng/dL). Sexes broadly similar.

free_t3.population_source: Approximate adult-population free T3, conventional reference ~3.5-6.5 pmol/L. No single definitive general-population PMID.

optimal.rationale: Low free T3 is a hallmark of non-thyroidal illness syndrome and strongly predicts frailty and mortality; mid-to-upper-normal free T3 is favorable. Conventional reference with risk skewed toward avoiding low values.
optimal.source: Conventional reference; non-thyroidal illness / low-T3 mortality literature

free_t3.note: Free T3 is heavily confounded by illness; 'higher_better' applies only within the physiological range and largely reflects illness severity rather than intrinsic thyroid function. Mean/SD approximate, assay-dependent.

reverse_t3.population_source: Natural-log of serum reverse T3; conventional reference ~10-24 ng/dL (ln ~2.3-3.2). Assays poorly standardized; no definitive general-population PMID.

optimal.rationale: Conventional reference range only; elevated rT3 marks catabolic/non-thyroidal illness state. No validated risk-optimal band exists. Lower-to-mid range preferred as it indexes absence of illness stress.
optimal.source: Conventional reference range (assay-dependent)

reverse_t3.note: rT3 assays are poorly standardized and rT3 is dominated by acute/chronic illness rather than intrinsic physiology. population_mean/sd are of the NATURAL LOG of ng/dL and are approximate/unverified. Optimal in raw ng/dL. Outcomes use tertile metrics only.

optimal.rationale: Thyroid antibodies are interpreted as positive/negative against an assay cutoff, not as a continuous risk gradient. Most of the population is at or near the assay floor, producing a non-normal, censored distribution.

thyroid_antibodies.note: Categorical (positive/negative) marker of thyroid autoimmunity, not a continuous quantitative biomarker; outcomes use categorical metrics. ~10-15% of adults (more in older women) are TPOAb-positive. No meaningful population mean/SD on a per-SD scale, so applicable:false.

total_testosterone.population_source: Men 19-39y harmonized reference, NHANES-based: 10.4-30.1 nmol/L (2.5-97.5th centile), mean ~15. Travison 2017 J Clin Endocrinol Metab PMID:28324103 (harmonized reference ranges).

optimal.rationale: U-shaped mortality association in men: both low and very high total testosterone associate with higher mortality; lowest-risk band is the mid-to-upper normal range. Low T marks frailty and metabolic disease.
optimal.source: Travison 2017 PMID:28324103; Yeap 2014 / testosterone-mortality cohorts

total_testosterone.note: Reference sex: MEN (women run ~0.5-2.4 nmol/L, ~20-30x lower; a separate distribution applies). Morning sample assumed. Declines with age and obesity. ng/dL = nmol/L x 28.84.

free_testosterone.population_source: Calculated free testosterone (Vermeulen equation) in men; healthy young-adult reference ~225-525 pmol/L (~65-150 pg/mL). Approximate from harmonized cohorts; assay/equation-dependent.

optimal.rationale: Low calculated free testosterone associates with frailty, dementia and mortality in men; mid-to-upper normal range is favorable. Conventional reference with risk skewed toward avoiding low values.
optimal.source: Conventional reference; Hsu 2018 / free testosterone-frailty-mortality literature

free_testosterone.note: Reference sex: MEN. Free T is calculated, not directly measured, so depends on SHBG assay and chosen equation; women's distribution is far lower and separate. Mean/SD approximate. pg/mL = pmol/L / 3.47.

shbg.population_source: Natural-log of serum SHBG; adult men reference ~18-54 nmol/L, geometric mean ~35 (ln ~3.6). Approximate from large cohorts (e.g. EMAS / NHANES-linked studies).

optimal.rationale: U-shaped associations: low SHBG marks insulin resistance/hepatic fat and metabolic syndrome, while very high SHBG accompanies aging and frailty. Mid-range is risk-minimizing.
optimal.source: Conventional reference; SHBG-metabolic-syndrome and SHBG-frailty literature

shbg.note: SHBG is right-skewed; population_mean/sd are of the NATURAL LOG of nmol/L (geometric mean ~35). Reference sex: MEN (women run higher, ~30-90 nmol/L). Optimal low/high in raw nmol/L. Raised by oral estrogen/thyroid hormone, lowered by insulin resistance.

estradiol.population_source: Natural-log of serum estradiol in postmenopausal women / older men (low-level range ~15-110 pmol/L). Premenopausal women have far higher, cycle-dependent levels. Approximate; LC-MS/MS-based cohorts.

optimal.rationale: Associations are strongly sex- and menopause-status-dependent; in postmenopausal women very high endogenous estradiol associates with breast cancer risk while extremely low levels associate with bone loss. No simple risk-minimizing band; conventional low-level reference given.
optimal.source: Conventional reference; Key 2002 (endogenous estradiol and breast cancer) PMID:12189224

estradiol.note: Reference sex/state: POSTMENOPAUSAL women (and similar low range in men). Premenopausal estradiol is highly cycle-dependent (~110-1500 pmol/L) and not represented here. population_mean/sd are of the NATURAL LOG of pmol/L. pg/mL = pmol/L / 3.67. Immunoassays unreliable at low levels.

optimal.rationale: Endogenous progesterone has extreme within-cycle variation (follicular <3 nmol/L vs luteal 15-90 nmol/L) and is near-undetectable in men and postmenopausal women; no stable population distribution applies for chronic-risk scaling.

progesterone.note: A single progesterone value is interpretable only when timed to cycle phase; there is no meaningful general-population mean/SD for a per-SD risk scale. Only one outcome (cancer) has a continuous metric. applicable:false.

lh.population_source: Natural-log of serum LH in adult men (reference ~1.7-8.6 IU/L, geometric mean ~5). Approximate; cycle- and menopause-dependent in women.

optimal.rationale: Conventional adult reference range only; rising LH signals declining gonadal feedback (aging/hypogonadism/menopause). No validated risk-optimal band for chronic outcomes; outcomes use categorical metrics.
optimal.source: Conventional clinical reference range

lh.note: Reference sex: MEN. LH is pulsatile (90-120 min pulses) so single values are noisy; in women it is highly cycle/menopause dependent (postmenopausal far higher). population_mean/sd are of the NATURAL LOG of IU/L. Approximate.

fsh.population_source: Adult men FSH reference ~1.5-12.4 IU/L. Approximate from clinical cohorts; rises sharply with gonadal aging.

optimal.rationale: Lower FSH within the reference range reflects intact gonadal function; high FSH marks gonadal aging and has been linked experimentally to bone loss and adiposity. Risk-minimizing band is the lower reference range.
optimal.source: Conventional reference; FSH-bone/CVD experimental literature

fsh.note: Reference sex: MEN (premenopausal women cycle-dependent; postmenopausal women run 25-135 IU/L, a wholly separate distribution). FSH is right-skewed but the per_sd outcome metric implies a linear analysis, so linear scale used. Mean/SD approximate.

igf1.population_source: Adult IGF-1 reference; strongly age-dependent. Approximate mid-adult (~40-50y) value from harmonized assays. Friedrich 2014 / Burgers 2011 meta-analysis of IGF-1 and mortality (PMID:21677044).

optimal.rationale: U-shaped mortality association: both low and high IGF-1 increase all-cause/CVD mortality; high IGF-1 raises cancer risk while low IGF-1 marks frailty. Lowest-risk trough is the mid-normal range.
optimal.source: Burgers 2011 PMID:21677044 (U-shaped IGF-1-mortality); Rahmani 2022 meta-analysis
