igf1.note: IGF-1 declines markedly with age, so the population mean is strongly age-dependent; value approximates mid-adult. Use age-specific (and ideally assay-specific) reference ranges. Sexes broadly similar. Mean/SD approximate.

igfbp3.population_source: Adult IGFBP-3 reference ~2-7 ug/mL (mg/L). Approximate mid-adult value from cohort studies; mildly age-dependent.

optimal.rationale: IGFBP-3 is interpreted mainly relative to IGF-1 (the IGF-1/IGFBP-3 ratio indexes free IGF-1); conventional mid-normal range given. Independent risk-optimal band is not well established.
optimal.source: Conventional reference; IGF/IGFBP-3 cohort literature

igfbp3.note: IGFBP-3 is biologically meaningful chiefly in the context of IGF-1; independent interpretation is limited. Mildly age-dependent. Mean/SD approximate. Sexes broadly similar.

optimal.rationale: A single random GH value carries little information because of pulsatile secretion (sleep peaks) and a ~15-minute half-life; assessment requires stimulation/suppression testing. No meaningful population distribution for a per-SD risk scale.

growth_hormone.note: Not a single quantitative measurement usable for risk scaling; GH is assessed by dynamic testing or via IGF-1 as a stable proxy. Outcomes use categorical metrics only. applicable:false.

melatonin.population_source: Natural-log of overnight urinary aMT6s (integrated nocturnal melatonin output); right-skewed, declines with age and light-at-night. Approximate from cohort studies (e.g. Nurses' Health Study aMT6s, Devore 2013).

optimal.rationale: Higher integrated nocturnal melatonin output is broadly favorable (lower CVD/cancer risk in cohort studies); low/mistimed melatonin indexes circadian disruption. Conventional upper-range preferred; no validated risk trough.
optimal.source: Conventional reference; Devore 2013 / aMT6s-outcome cohort literature

melatonin.note: Reported as integrated overnight urinary aMT6s; a single daytime plasma/saliva value is uninformative. population_mean/sd are of the NATURAL LOG of the aMT6s concentration and are approximate; absolute values depend heavily on collection protocol and units. Declines with age.

horvath_clock.population_source: Mean 0 by construction (residual of DNAm age on chronological age). Typical SD of Horvath intrinsic/extrinsic age acceleration ~4-6 yr; e.g. Marioni 2015, Genome Biol, PMID:25633388.

optimal.rationale: Negative age acceleration (epigenetically younger than calendar age) is associated with lower mortality; values <=0 are favorable. No risk-minimizing trough is well established.
optimal.source: Chen 2016, Aging, PMID:27595879

horvath_clock.note: Horvath age acceleration is a residual with mean 0 by construction, not an absolute quantity. A consumer cannot compare a raw 'value' to a population mean; only the residual relative to their own chronological age is meaningful, and it is lab/array/cell-composition dependent. SD is approximate and cohort-specific. Not scorable as a standalone population-referenced biomarker.

hannum_clock.population_source: Mean 0 by construction. Typical SD of Hannum age acceleration ~4-6 yr; Marioni 2015, Genome Biol, PMID:25633388.

optimal.rationale: Lower (negative) age acceleration associated with lower mortality.
optimal.source: Chen 2016, Aging, PMID:27595879

hannum_clock.note: Residual with mean 0 by construction; lab/array/cell-composition dependent. Not scorable as a population-referenced absolute value.

phenoage_clock.population_source: Mean 0 by construction. SD of PhenoAgeAccel typically ~5-6 yr; Levine 2018, Aging, PMID:29676998.

optimal.rationale: Negative PhenoAge acceleration (younger phenotypic age than calendar age) tracks lower mortality and disease risk; <=0 favorable.
optimal.source: Levine 2018, Aging, PMID:29676998

phenoage_clock.note: Second-generation clock but still reported as an age-acceleration residual (mean 0 by construction). Not scorable against a population mean as an absolute value; SD is cohort/array dependent.

grimage_clock.units: years (GrimAge / GrimAge2 age acceleration residual, AgeAccelGrim)
grimage_clock.population_source: Mean 0 by construction. SD of AgeAccelGrim typically ~4-5 yr; Lu 2019, Aging, PMID:30669119.

optimal.rationale: GrimAge acceleration is the strongest mortality predictor among DNAm clocks; negative acceleration favorable, <=0 is the low-risk direction.
optimal.source: Lu 2019, Aging, PMID:30669119

grimage_clock.note: Age-acceleration residual with mean 0 by construction; lab/array dependent. Not scorable as an absolute population-referenced value.

dunedinpace_clock.units: years of biological aging per calendar year (pace)
dunedinpace_clock.population_source: DunedinPACE is normalized to ~1.0 (one year of aging per calendar year) in the Dunedin reference cohort at age 45; SD ~0.09-0.16 across cohorts. Belsky 2022, eLife, PMID:35029144.

optimal.rationale: Pace below 1.0 means aging slower than calendar time; lower pace is associated with lower mortality, CVD and dementia risk. Risk-minimizing direction is lower, with ~0.7-1.0 a realistic favorable band.
optimal.source: Belsky 2022, eLife, PMID:35029144

dunedinpace_clock.note: Unlike age-acceleration clocks, DunedinPACE has an interpretable absolute scale (~1.0 = pace of calendar aging) so it is scorable. But absolute values shift with age, array batch and normalization; SD is approximate and cohort-dependent. Treat per-SD placement as indicative.

optimal.rationale: Longer leukocyte telomere length is generally associated with lower mortality (per-SD shorter = higher risk), but no assay-independent risk-optimal range exists.
optimal.source: Rode 2015, J Natl Cancer Inst, PMID:25862531

telomere_length.note: Leukocyte telomere length is strongly assay-dependent (qPCR T/S ratio vs Southern blot kb vs flow-FISH are not interchangeable) and not standardized across labs. No universal population mean/SD a consumer can compare against. Values are only interpretable as age-/sex-adjusted percentiles within a single lab's assay. Not scorable.

optimal.rationale: Glycan age above chronological age (excess >3 yr) is associated with higher cardiovascular risk; lower glycan age relative to calendar age favorable.
optimal.source: Kristic 2014, J Gerontol A, PMID:24336995

glycanage.note: GlycanAge is a proprietary biological-age estimate interpreted relative to chronological age (a gap), not an absolute population-scale measurement. No published consumer-comparable population mean/SD of the raw score. Not scorable.

proteomic_age_clock.population_source: Mean 0 by construction (residual of proteomic age on chronological age). SD of ProtAgeGap ~6-8 yr; Argentieri 2024, Nat Med, PMID:39117961.

optimal.rationale: Negative proteomic age gap (proteomically younger) is associated with lower mortality and disease risk; <=0 favorable.
optimal.source: Argentieri 2024, Nat Med, PMID:39117961

proteomic_age_clock.note: Age-gap residual with mean 0 by construction; platform-specific (Olink vs SomaScan) and model-specific. Not scorable as an absolute population-referenced value; SD is approximate.

dexa_body_fat_pct.population_source: NHANES 1999-2004 DXA whole-body, US adults 20+. Mean DXA total body fat is ~28% in men and ~40% in women; pooled adult ~31-34%, SD ~7-9%. Kelly 2009, PLoS One, PMID:19753111.

optimal.rationale: Mortality vs body fat % is U-shaped; lowest-risk trough is roughly 10-22% in men and ~23-35% in women. Sex-pooled low-risk band ~10-25% (men) / ~20-35% (women). Use sex-specific ranges in practice.
optimal.source: Padwal 2016, Ann Intern Med, PMID:26954388

dexa_body_fat_pct.note: Strongly sex-dependent; dashboard should use sex-specific mean/SD (men ~25-28%, women ~38-40%). Pooled values given here are approximate. DXA values are device/manufacturer dependent.

visceral_adipose_tissue.units: cm2 (VAT cross-sectional area, CT/MRI) or g (DXA VAT mass)
visceral_adipose_tissue.population_source: CT-measured VAT area, Framingham Heart Study, US adults: mean ~140 cm2 men / ~95 cm2 women; pooled ~100-120 cm2, SD ~50-60 cm2. Maurovich-Horvat 2007 / Fox 2007, Circulation, PMID:17576866.

optimal.rationale: Higher VAT raises mortality, CVD and cancer risk monotonically in most data; lower is better. A VAT area below ~100 cm2 (CT) is a commonly used low-risk threshold.
optimal.source: Fox 2007, Circulation, PMID:17576866

visceral_adipose_tissue.note: Units differ by modality (CT/MRI cm2 vs DXA grams vs BIA estimate) and are not interchangeable; values given are for CT cross-sectional area at L4-L5. Strongly sex-dependent (men > women). Use modality- and sex-specific references.

bone_mineral_density.population_source: T-score is defined as SD from the young healthy adult mean (NHANES III femoral-neck reference, non-Hispanic white women 20-29). By definition the young-adult reference mean is 0, SD 1. Looker 1998, Osteoporos Int, PMID:9797906.

optimal.rationale: WHO criteria: T-score >= -1.0 is normal bone density; lower mortality and fracture/frailty risk. Osteoporosis at <= -2.5. Higher BMD is protective.
optimal.source: WHO 1994 / Kanis 2008, Osteoporos Int, PMID:18266020

bone_mineral_density.note: Reported as T-score (already standardized to young-adult SD units); a consumer's T-score is directly on a per-SD scale by construction. An age-matched older population would have a mean well below 0 (older adults lose bone), so T-score 0 represents the young-adult reference, not the age-peer mean. Outcome HRs in the source are per-SD lower.

waist_circumference.population_source: NHANES 2015-2018, US adults 20+: mean waist circumference ~102 cm men, ~98 cm women; pooled ~99-100 cm, SD ~15-17 cm. Fryar 2021, NCHS / CDC anthropometric reference data, DOI:10.15620/cdc:100478.

optimal.rationale: Lower-risk threshold ~94 cm (men) / ~80 cm (women) per IDF/WHO; mortality rises with higher waist circumference adjusted for BMI. Sex-pooled favorable upper bound ~88-94 cm.
optimal.source: WHO 2008 waist circumference report; Cerhan 2014, Mayo Clin Proc, PMID:24582763

waist_circumference.note: Strongly sex-dependent; use sex-specific thresholds (men 94/102 cm, women 80/88 cm action levels). Pooled mean/SD given. Measurement-site protocol affects values.

waist_hip_ratio.population_source: NHANES / pooled cohort data, US adults: mean WHR ~0.95 men, ~0.87 women; pooled ~0.90, SD ~0.07-0.09. WHO 2008 waist circumference and waist-hip ratio report.

optimal.rationale: WHO substantially-increased-risk cutoffs are >=0.90 men and >=0.85 women; mortality and CVD rise with higher WHR even adjusted for BMI. Lower is better; favorable upper bound ~0.85-0.90 sex-dependent.
optimal.source: WHO 2008 waist circumference and waist-hip ratio report; Pischon 2008, NEJM, PMID:19005195

waist_hip_ratio.note: Sex-dependent; use sex-specific cutoffs (men >=0.90, women >=0.85). Pooled mean/SD given as approximate.

bmi.population_source: NHANES 2017-2018, US adults 20+: mean BMI ~29.5 kg/m2, SD ~7 kg/m2. Fryar 2021, NCHS anthropometric reference data, DOI:10.15620/cdc:100478.

optimal.rationale: All-cause mortality is U-shaped with a trough around BMI 22-25 in never-smokers without prevalent disease; both obesity (>=30) and underweight (<18.5-20) raise mortality and frailty risk. The 22-25 band is the low-risk trough.
optimal.source: Global BMI Mortality Collaboration, Di Angelantonio 2016, Lancet, PMID:27423262

bmi.note: U-shaped mortality association; optimal is a trough range, not a one-sided threshold. US population mean (~29.5) sits in the overweight range. Trough shifts slightly upward at older ages.

bioimpedance_phase_angle.population_source: Pooled BIA reference data, general adult population: mean phase angle ~6.0-6.5 degrees (higher in men, declines with age), SD ~0.9-1.1. Bosy-Westphal 2006, JPEN, PMID:17047168.

optimal.rationale: Higher phase angle is associated with lower all-cause mortality and better cell-membrane integrity / muscle quality; no upper-risk bound. Values above the age/sex-specific median (~6 degrees) are favorable.
optimal.source: Wirth 2010 / Norman 2012, Clin Nutr, PMID:22188785

bioimpedance_phase_angle.note: Strongly age-, sex- and device/frequency-dependent (declines with age, lower in women). Pooled mean/SD approximate; dashboard should use age/sex-specific BIA-device references. Single- vs multi-frequency devices not interchangeable.

total_sleep_time.population_source: NHANES self-reported sleep duration, US adults: mean ~7.0-7.1 h, SD ~1.2-1.5 h. Sheehan 2019, Sleep, PMID:30452725.

optimal.rationale: All-cause mortality and dementia are U-shaped with sleep duration; lowest risk at ~7-8 h. Both short (<7h) and long (>9h) sleep raise risk.
optimal.source: Cappuccio 2010, Sleep, PMID:20469800; Yin 2017, J Am Heart Assoc, PMID:28889101

total_sleep_time.note: Self-reported total sleep time overestimates objective (actigraphy/PSG) duration by ~0.5-1 h; mean/SD given are for self-report (most large mortality cohorts). U-shaped association: optimal is a trough range.

sleep_efficiency.population_source: Actigraphy/PSG-derived sleep efficiency in adult cohorts: mean ~85-90%, SD ~6-9%. Mander 2017 / Ohayon 2004 normative meta-analysis, Sleep, PMID:15586779.

optimal.rationale: Sleep efficiency >=90% (or >93%) is conventionally 'good'; the lowest actigraphic quartile (<=85%) shows higher mortality. Higher is better up to ~100%.
optimal.source: Ohayon 2004, Sleep, PMID:15586779; Dew 2003, Psychosom Med, PMID:12554815

sleep_efficiency.note: Strongly method-dependent (PSG vs actigraphy vs consumer wearable estimates differ); declines with age. Mean/SD approximate and method-mixed. Treat per-SD placement cautiously.

deep_sleep_duration.units: % of total sleep (N3 slow-wave sleep) or minutes
deep_sleep_duration.population_source: PSG normative meta-analysis, healthy adults: N3/slow-wave sleep ~13-23% of total sleep, declining strongly with age; pooled adult ~15%, SD ~6-8%. Ohayon 2004, Sleep, PMID:15586779.

optimal.rationale: Lower slow-wave sleep is associated with higher mortality, hypertension and dementia; the normal healthy-adult range ~13-23% is the favorable band. More is generally better within physiological range.
optimal.source: Ohayon 2004, Sleep, PMID:15586779; conventional reference (no risk-minimizing trough established)

deep_sleep_duration.note: Strongly age-dependent (slow-wave sleep declines markedly with age) so a single population mean is a rough average; dashboard should use age-specific references. Consumer wearable 'deep sleep' estimates agree poorly with PSG N3 and are not directly comparable.

rem_sleep_duration.population_source: PSG normative meta-analysis, healthy adults: REM ~18-23% of total sleep; pooled ~20-22%, SD ~5-7%. Ohayon 2004, Sleep, PMID:15586779.

optimal.rationale: Lower REM percentage is associated with higher all-cause, cardiovascular and dementia risk (per 5% lower REM = higher risk); the normal ~20-25% band is favorable. Higher within physiological range is better.
optimal.source: Leary 2020, JAMA Neurol, PMID:32628261 (MrOS/Wisconsin); conventional PSG reference

rem_sleep_duration.note: Method-dependent (PSG vs wearable HRV-based estimates); modestly age-dependent. Mean/SD approximate. Wearable REM estimates have moderate accuracy and are not interchangeable with PSG.

optimal.rationale: AHI <5 = no/minimal OSA and the reference low-risk category; severe OSA (AHI >=30) carries substantially higher mortality, MACE and dementia risk. Lower is better. Conventional clinical thresholds (no risk-minimizing trough).
optimal.source: AASM scoring criteria; Marshall 2014 / Punjabi 2009, PLoS Med, PMID:19688045

sleep_apnea_severity.note: AHI/ODI are heavily right-skewed count variables, not normally distributed, and depend strongly on the hypopnea scoring rule (3% vs 4% desaturation) and recording modality (in-lab PSG vs home test). A population mean/SD a consumer can compare per-SD against is not meaningful; OSA is graded categorically (<5 none, 5-15 mild, 15-30 moderate, >=30 severe). Not scorable on a per-SD scale; use clinical severity bands.

sleep_regularity.population_source: UK Biobank accelerometry-derived Sleep Regularity Index, ~60,000 adults: distribution centered ~70-72, with quintile cutoffs roughly 60 / 71.6 / 79 / 87.3. Windred 2024, Sleep, PMID:37889880.

optimal.rationale: Most regular sleepers (highest SRI quintile, SRI >~87) have the lowest all-cause mortality and CVD risk; irregular sleepers (SRI <~71.6) have elevated risk. Higher SRI is better.
optimal.source: Windred 2024, Sleep, PMID:37889880

sleep_regularity.note: SRI is an index (0 = fully irregular, 100 = perfectly regular), not a physical measurement; mean/SD derived from UK Biobank accelerometry quintile cutoffs and are device/algorithm-dependent. Other regularity metrics (SD of sleep onset/duration) are not interchangeable with SRI. Scorable but treat per-SD placement as approximate.

psa.population_source: Age-dependent. NHANES 2001-2004 men with no prostate-cancer history: median total PSA ~0.7 ng/mL at age 40-49, ~1.0 at 50-59, ~1.5 at 60-69, ~2.1 at 70+ (Lim 2010; Tang 2010 NHANES analyses). PSA is markedly right-skewed, so the geometric mean (~0.9-1.1 ng/mL for men 40-59) is more representative than the arithmetic mean; the SD shown is an approximate spread on the raw ng/mL scale and is itself age-dependent.

optimal.rationale: Not a treat-to-target biomarker. For long-term risk stratification, a single mid-life (age 40-55) PSA at or below the age-specific median (roughly <=1.0 ng/mL at 40-49, <=1.0-1.5 at 50-59) identifies men with very low long-term risk of lethal prostate cancer; the top decile at age 40-49 carries an ~8.7-fold odds of lethal prostate cancer over ~30 years. Diagnostic biopsy thresholds (commonly ~3-4 ng/mL) are a separate clinical-action concept and are age- and assay-dependent.
optimal.source: Preston MA 2016 J Clin Oncol (PMID:27298404); Vickers AJ 2013 BMJ (PMID:23596126)

psa.note: PSA is prostate- not cancer-specific: benign prostatic hyperplasia, prostatitis, recent ejaculation/cycling/DRE/instrumentation raise it, while 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors roughly halve it and obesity lowers measured values by hemodilution. Reference values are strongly age-dependent and the distribution is right-skewed (hence scale=log); a dashboard should compare against an age-specific reference, not a single mean. Population mean/SD shown are approximate composites from NHANES-based analyses, not a single primary statistic. Applies to people with a prostate.

mced_galleri.units: binary call (cancer-signal detected / not detected, plus predicted tissue of origin)
mced_galleri.population_source: Not a continuous measurement with a population mean/SD. In the PATHFINDER cohort a cancer signal was detected in ~1.4% (92/6621) of adults age 50+.

optimal.rationale: No continuous range exists. The desirable result is 'no cancer signal detected'; a 'signal detected' result triggers tissue-of-origin-directed diagnostic workup.
optimal.source: Schrag D 2023 Lancet (PMID:37805216); Klein EA 2021 Ann Oncol (PMID:34176681)

mced_galleri.note: Galleri is a multi-cancer early-detection screening test, not a quantitative aging biomarker. It returns a binary cancer-signal-detected/not-detected call plus a predicted tissue of origin, not a number on a scale, so there is no population mean/SD to score a person against. A dashboard should display it as a test status (detected / not detected) with the date performed and, if positive, the predicted tissue of origin and a recommendation for confirmatory workup; sensitivity is low for early-stage disease (~17% stage I) and no completed trial shows it reduces cancer mortality.

screening_compliance.units: categorical adherence status (up-to-date vs overdue per guideline-recommended screen)
screening_compliance.population_source: Not a biological measurement; it is an adherence behaviour. No population mean/SD applies.

optimal.rationale: The desirable state is being up-to-date with every age- and risk-appropriate screen (e.g. colorectal, breast, cervical, lung where eligible). This is a checklist/status, not a continuous optimal band.
optimal.source: USPSTF screening recommendations; Bretthauer M 2023 JAMA Intern Med (PMID:37639247)

screening_compliance.note: Screening compliance is a behavioural composite (up-to-date vs overdue for each guideline-recommended cancer screen), not a single quantitative measurement, so there is no population mean/SD to score against. A dashboard should display it as a per-screen checklist (each screen: up-to-date / due / not-eligible, with next-due date) or a simple completed-fraction, rather than a standardized score. Observational mortality associations are heavily confounded by the healthy-screenee effect.

mthfr_variants.units: genotype (CC / CT / TT for C677T rs1801133; optionally A1298C)
mthfr_variants.population_source: A fixed genotype, not a continuous measurement. Allele frequency is ancestry-dependent (TT homozygote frequency roughly 10-15% in European, higher in some Hispanic/East Asian populations, lower in African-ancestry populations).

optimal.rationale: There is no optimal value for a genotype. Risk contrasts are categorical (TT vs CC) and folate-status-dependent; the actionable downstream phenotype is homocysteine, which is covered by its own biomarker.
optimal.source: Klerk M 2002 JAMA (PMID:12387655); Yang Q 2012 Am J Clin Nutr (PMID:22492374)

mthfr_variants.note: MTHFR C677T is a fixed germline genotype (CC/CT/TT), not a quantitative measurement, so there is no mean/SD or optimal range to score against. A dashboard should display the genotype itself plus a plain-language interpretation (TT homozygotes have ~25-30% higher plasma homocysteine when folate is low) and direct attention to the modifiable downstream marker, homocysteine, and to adequate folate/B12/B6 intake. The CHD association is weak and conflicting once publication bias is corrected.

pharmacogenomic_panel.units: star-allele diplotypes and metabolizer phenotypes (poor / intermediate / normal / rapid / ultrarapid) per gene
pharmacogenomic_panel.population_source: A multi-gene fixed genotype panel, not a continuous measurement. Star-allele and metabolizer-phenotype frequencies are ancestry-dependent and gene-specific.

optimal.rationale: No optimal value exists; the panel predicts drug response and adverse-reaction risk, which is conditional on which medications a person is prescribed.
optimal.source: Swen JJ 2023 Lancet, PREPARE trial (doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(22)01841-4)

pharmacogenomic_panel.note: A pharmacogenomic panel is a set of fixed drug-metabolism genotypes, not a quantitative biomarker, so there is no population mean/SD or optimal range. A dashboard should display per-gene metabolizer phenotypes (e.g. CYP2C19 poor metabolizer, CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizer) with the relevant drug-gene guidance, surfaced at the point of prescribing rather than scored as a longevity metric.

polygenic_risk_scores.units: standardized polygenic score (SD units, per disease; e.g. CAD, breast cancer, Alzheimer's)
polygenic_risk_scores.population_source: A PRS is by construction standardized to its reference population (mean 0, SD 1 in the ancestry-matched derivation/reference cohort), so there is no external mean/SD to add; the meaningful output is the individual's percentile within an ancestry-matched reference.

optimal.rationale: There is no optimal value for a fixed germline score. Each disease-specific PRS is a separate continuous risk score; per-SD HRs (e.g. ~1.7 for incident CAD with GPS-Mult, ~1.6 for invasive breast cancer with PRS313) describe relative risk, not a target band.
optimal.source: Patel AP 2023 Nat Med (PMID:37414900); Mavaddat N 2019 Am J Hum Genet (PMID:30554720); Levin MG 2021 Circ Genom Precis Med (PMID:34670400)

polygenic_risk_scores.note: Polygenic risk scores are fixed germline susceptibility scores reported as a percentile or SD-rank within an ancestry-matched reference population, not a single measurable biomarker with one population mean/SD a person can be scored against. There is also no single PRS: each disease (CAD, breast cancer, Alzheimer's) is a separate score with its own scale, version and ancestry-dependent calibration. A dashboard should display each disease-specific PRS as a percentile within an ancestry-matched reference, with the per-SD hazard ratio for context, and emphasize that the actionable response is earlier/intensified control of modifiable risk factors.
polygenic_risk_scores._name: Polygenic risk scores (CAD, breast cancer, Alzheimer's)
